Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples and detailed exam-focused practice.

question: A student states that every vector quantity must always be written with a compass direction such as north, south, east or west. Which of the following observations best shows that the student's statement is incorrect?

options.A: A force of 25 N acting vertically upward is a vector even though no compass direction is used.
options.B: A distance of 50 m due east is a vector because east is a compass direction.
options.C: A speed of 30 m s⁻¹ due north is a vector because north is specified.
options.D: A temperature of 45 °C is a vector because it can increase or decrease.

answer_text: A force of 25 N acting vertically upward is a vector even though no compass direction is used.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests whether you understand what actually makes a physical quantity a vector. Specifically, it examines whether a vector must always be expressed using compass directions such as north, south, east or west, or whether any specified direction is sufficient.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us first understand what a vector quantity is.

A physical quantity is any measurable property that can be expressed using a numerical value and a unit. Physical quantities are divided into two broad classes:

• Scalar quantities
• Vector quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a vector quantity?'

A vector quantity is a physical quantity that requires BOTH:

• magnitude (the numerical size together with its unit), and
• direction.

A beginner may also ask, 'What exactly is direction?'

Direction simply tells where a quantity acts or points. Many beginners think direction must always be written as north, south, east or west. This is not true.

Direction may be expressed in many different ways, including:

• north, south, east or west;
• upward or downward;
• left or right;
• towards or away from an object;
• at an angle such as 30° above the horizontal or 45° south of east.

As long as the direction is clearly stated, the quantity can be completely described.

For example:

• A force of 15 N upward is a vector.
• A velocity of 20 m s⁻¹ due east is a vector.
• An acceleration of 4 m s⁻² downward is a vector.

Notice that 'upward' and 'downward' are not compass directions, yet they still specify direction.

Therefore, a quantity does not need compass directions to qualify as a vector. It only needs a clearly defined direction.

Another beginner may ask, 'Can adding a direction turn any quantity into a vector?'

No. A scalar quantity remains a scalar even if someone incorrectly attaches a direction to it. For example, saying '30 m s⁻¹ due north' is still incorrect if the quantity is speed because speed, by definition, does not include direction. Once direction is included, the correct quantity becomes velocity, not speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the student's claim.

The student believes that every vector must be written using compass directions such as north, south, east or west.

Step 2: Recall the definition of a vector.

A vector requires magnitude and ANY valid direction. Physics does not restrict direction to compass points alone.

Step 3: Examine Option A.

'A force of 25 N acting vertically upward.'

The magnitude is 25 N.

The direction is vertically upward.

Although 'upward' is not a compass direction, it is still a valid direction.

Therefore, this statement disproves the student's claim.

Step 4: Examine Option B.

Distance is a scalar quantity. Adding the words 'due east' does not change distance into a vector. The correct vector quantity would be displacement.

Step 5: Examine Option C.

Speed is a scalar quantity. Once direction is included, the correct physical quantity should be velocity. Therefore, this statement is scientifically incorrect.

Step 6: Examine Option D.

Temperature is always a scalar quantity. The fact that it may increase or decrease does not make it a vector because it does not require direction.
Step 7: Therefore, the statement that correctly disproves the student's claim is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because distance is defined as the total length of the path travelled and is a scalar quantity. Merely attaching a compass direction does not convert distance into displacement. JAMB often uses this as a conceptual trap.
Option C is incorrect because speed is a scalar quantity. The phrase 'due north' belongs to velocity, not speed. Candidates who confuse speed with velocity frequently choose this option.
Option D is incorrect because temperature measures the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It has magnitude only and never requires direction, even when its value changes.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests your understanding of the meaning of 'direction.' Remember that a vector does not require compass directions specifically. Any valid directional description—such as upward, downward, left, right, towards, away, or a stated angle—is sufficient. Also beware of questions that deliberately attach a direction to scalar quantities such as speed or distance. Adding a direction does not change the physical definition of the quantity.
explanation.final_answer: A. A force of 25 N acting vertically upward is a vector even though no compass direction is used.
explanation.simple_explanation:
A vector needs two things: a size and a direction.

The direction does not have to be north or south. Words like 'upward', 'downward', 'to the left', 'to the right' or 'at 30°' are also directions.

For example, a force of 25 N upward is a complete vector because it tells both how large the force is and where it acts.

Therefore, the student's statement is wrong because vectors can use many different kinds of directions, not only compass directions.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Quantities to learn how vectors are represented, the different ways direction can be expressed, common JAMB misconceptions about vectors, and additional worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, detailed explanations, and exam-focused practice questions.

question: A student argues that speed and velocity are both measured in m s⁻¹, so they must both be vector quantities. Which statement best explains why the student's conclusion is incorrect?

options.A: Velocity is a vector because it specifies both magnitude and direction, whereas speed gives only the magnitude of motion.
options.B: Speed is not a vector because it is measured in m s⁻², while velocity is measured in m s⁻¹.
options.C: Velocity is a vector because it always has a greater numerical value than speed.
options.D: Speed is not a vector because it can only be measured in the laboratory.

answer_text: Velocity is a vector because it specifies both magnitude and direction, whereas speed gives only the magnitude of motion.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests whether you understand that the classification of a physical quantity as a scalar or a vector depends on the information needed to describe it completely, not on the unit used to measure it. It also checks whether you can distinguish between speed and velocity, which JAMB frequently tests.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with measurable quantities called physical quantities. Every physical quantity has a magnitude. Magnitude simply means the size or numerical value of the quantity together with its unit.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?' A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude alone. Once you know its numerical value and unit, no further information is needed. Examples include mass, temperature, energy, distance, time and speed.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is a vector quantity?' A vector quantity is a physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction before it is fully described. Direction tells where the quantity acts or points. Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum and weight.

A common misunderstanding is to think that quantities with the same unit must belong to the same category. This is not true. The unit tells us what is being measured, but it does not tell us whether the quantity is scalar or vector.

For example, both speed and velocity are measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹). However, speed tells only how fast an object moves, while velocity tells both how fast the object moves and the direction of its motion. Therefore, velocity is a vector whereas speed is a scalar.

Always remember this rule:

• Magnitude only → Scalar.
• Magnitude + Direction → Vector.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantities being compared.

The question compares speed and velocity.

Step 2: Recall the definitions.

Speed is the distance travelled per unit time. It tells only how fast an object moves.

Velocity is the displacement per unit time. It tells both how fast the object moves and the direction of motion.

Step 3: Ask yourself whether direction is required.

To describe a speed of 20 m s⁻¹ completely, no direction is needed.

However, saying a car has a velocity of 20 m s⁻¹ is incomplete unless a direction such as east, west, north or south is added.

Step 4: Examine the options carefully.

Option A correctly states that velocity requires both magnitude and direction, while speed requires only magnitude.

Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because speed is also measured in m s⁻¹, not m s⁻². The unit m s⁻² belongs to acceleration.

Option C is incorrect because velocity is not necessarily greater than speed. In many situations, they may even have the same numerical value. Their difference lies in the presence or absence of direction, not in their magnitude.
Option D is incorrect because speed can be measured anywhere—for example, by a vehicle's speedometer on a road. Whether a quantity is scalar or vector has nothing to do with where it is measured.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently presents pairs of quantities with identical units, such as speed and velocity or distance and displacement, to test whether candidates understand the difference between scalar and vector quantities. Never classify a quantity based on its unit alone. Instead, ask yourself: 'Do I need a direction to describe this quantity completely?' If the answer is yes, it is a vector; otherwise, it is a scalar.
explanation.final_answer: A. Velocity is a vector because it specifies both magnitude and direction, whereas speed gives only the magnitude of motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two drivers.

Driver A says, 'I am travelling at 60 km/h.' You know how fast the car is moving, but you do not know where it is going. That is speed, which is a scalar.

Driver B says, 'I am travelling at 60 km/h due north.' Now you know both the speed and the direction. That is velocity, which is a vector.

Although both are measured in the same unit, only velocity includes direction. Therefore, velocity is a vector while speed is a scalar.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Quantities to learn how to distinguish vectors from scalars, compare commonly confused quantities such as distance/displacement and speed/velocity, and practise additional JAMB-style questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed beginner-friendly lessons and exam-focused practice.

question:
A student lists the following physical quantities during a Physics practical:

I. Temperature
II. Energy
III. Force
IV. Speed

Which combination consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question tests your ability to recognize which physical quantities are scalars and distinguish them from vectors. You are required to identify the quantities that have magnitude only and do not require a direction for complete description.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us understand what a physical quantity is.

A physical quantity is any measurable property of an object or phenomenon. Examples include mass, length, force, speed, temperature and energy.

Physical quantities are classified into two major groups:

1. Scalar quantities
2. Vector quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?'

A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or numerical value) together with its unit. It does not require any direction.

For example:
• A temperature of 30 °C is complete because no direction is needed.
• A mass of 5 kg is complete because it tells us how much matter is present.
• An energy of 500 J is complete because energy has no direction.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?'

Magnitude simply means the size, amount or numerical value of a quantity. For example, in '20 kg', the number 20 represents the magnitude while kilogram (kg) is the unit.

A beginner may also ask, 'What is a vector quantity?'

A vector quantity requires both magnitude and direction before it is completely described.

For example:
• A force of 10 N is incomplete because we must know whether it acts north, south, upward or downward.
• A velocity of 25 m s⁻¹ is incomplete until its direction is stated.

Now examine each quantity carefully.

Temperature measures the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It has magnitude only and therefore is a scalar.

Energy is the capacity to do work. Energy has magnitude but no direction. Therefore, it is also a scalar.

Force is a push or pull that can change the state of motion of a body. Since force acts in a particular direction, it is a vector.

Speed is the distance travelled per unit time. It tells us only how fast an object moves and not the direction of motion. Therefore, speed is a scalar.

Notice that students often confuse speed with velocity. Velocity includes direction, while speed does not.
steps:
Step 1: Identify Quantity I.

Temperature has magnitude only. Therefore, it is a scalar.

Step 2: Identify Quantity II.

Energy has magnitude only and does not act in any direction. Therefore, it is also a scalar.

Step 3: Identify Quantity III.

Force requires both magnitude and direction because a push or pull always acts in a particular direction. Therefore, force is a vector and should not be included.

Step 4: Identify Quantity IV.

Speed tells only how fast an object moves. Since no direction is involved, speed is a scalar.

Step 5: Collect all the scalar quantities.

The scalar quantities are Temperature (I), Energy (II) and Speed (IV).

Hence, the correct answer is I, II and IV only.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (I, III and IV only) is incorrect because Force (III) is a vector quantity. Many students mistakenly think force is a scalar because it is measured with a single number and unit, forgetting that its direction is equally important.
Option C (II and III only) is incorrect because although Energy (II) is a scalar, Force (III) is a vector. The combination therefore does not consist entirely of scalar quantities.
Option D (III and IV only) is incorrect because Force (III) is a vector while Speed (IV) is a scalar. The two quantities belong to different classes.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests your ability to distinguish similar-looking quantities. A common trap is confusing speed with velocity and distance with displacement. Remember:

• Scalars have magnitude only.
• Vectors have both magnitude and direction.

Whenever you see quantities such as force, displacement, velocity, acceleration, momentum or weight, immediately think 'vector'. Quantities such as mass, time, temperature, speed, energy, work, power and distance are scalars.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of a scalar as a quantity that only answers the question 'How much?'

Temperature tells how hot something is.
Energy tells how much energy is available.
Speed tells how fast something moves.

None of these needs a direction.

Force is different because you must know where it acts. A force of 20 N is not complete until you say whether it acts upward, downward, east or west. Therefore, force is a vector, not a scalar.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalars to learn how to identify scalar quantities, distinguish them from vectors, avoid common JAMB traps such as Speed vs Velocity and Distance vs Displacement, and practise additional worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more beginner-friendly, exam-focused lessons and practice questions.

question: A laboratory assistant records the following physical quantities during an experiment: temperature, force, mass and displacement. Which pair consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question requires you to identify, from a list of commonly measured physical quantities, the two that are scalar quantities. To answer correctly, you must know the difference between scalar and vector quantities rather than simply memorizing examples.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with quantities that describe the properties of objects and events. These quantities are grouped into two broad classes: scalar quantities and vector quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a physical quantity?' A physical quantity is anything that can be measured and expressed using a number together with a unit. For example, 10 kg, 25 °C, 5 m and 12 N are all physical quantities because they have numerical values and units.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?' A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or amount) only. It does not require a direction. If you know its numerical value and unit, you have all the information needed.

Examples of scalar quantities include mass, time, temperature, distance, speed, energy, work, power, volume, density and electric charge.

A beginner may then ask, 'What is a vector quantity?' A vector quantity is a physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction for its complete description. Simply stating the numerical value is not enough.

Examples of vector quantities include force, displacement, velocity, acceleration, momentum and weight.

Notice the important distinction between similar quantities. Distance is a scalar because it only tells how much ground has been covered, whereas displacement is a vector because it tells both how far an object is from its starting point and in what direction. Similarly, speed is a scalar while velocity is a vector.

JAMB frequently tests candidates by mixing scalar and vector quantities that appear closely related. Therefore, understanding their meanings is much more reliable than trying to memorize long lists.
steps: Step 1: Examine each quantity given in the question individually.

Step 2: Temperature measures the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It has magnitude only (for example, 35 °C). No direction is attached to temperature. Therefore, temperature is a scalar quantity.

Step 3: Force describes a push or pull acting on an object. To fully describe a force, both its magnitude and the direction in which it acts must be known. For example, '20 N east' completely describes a force, while '20 N' alone is incomplete. Therefore, force is a vector quantity.
Step 4: Mass is the amount of matter contained in an object. It is fully described by its numerical value and unit, such as 8 kg. No direction is involved. Therefore, mass is a scalar quantity.

Step 5: Displacement describes the straight-line change in position from one point to another together with the direction of that change. For example, '12 m north' is a displacement. Since direction is essential, displacement is a vector quantity.
Step 6: Identify the pair that contains only scalar quantities. Temperature and mass are both scalars, making Option C the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (Temperature and force): Temperature is a scalar, but force is a vector because its direction determines its effect. Since one quantity is scalar and the other is vector, the pair is incorrect.

Option B (Mass and displacement): Mass is a scalar, but displacement is a vector because it specifies both distance from the starting point and direction. Therefore, this pair does not consist entirely of scalars.
Option D (Force and displacement): Both force and displacement are vector quantities. This option contains no scalar quantity at all, making it incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB often pairs similar-looking quantities to test conceptual understanding rather than memorization. Learn these common pairs: distance–displacement, speed–velocity, mass–weight and work–force. Remember that if direction is required before the quantity is completely described, it is a vector. If direction is unnecessary, it is a scalar.
explanation.final_answer: C. Temperature and mass
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of a scalar as a quantity that only answers the question 'How much?'. For example, saying the mass of a bag is 6 kg gives complete information because no direction is needed. Temperature also needs only a value such as 30 °C. However, force and displacement must include a direction like east, west, north or south before they are fully described. Therefore, temperature and mass are the only two scalar quantities in the options.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalars and Vectors to learn how to distinguish scalar and vector quantities using memory aids, everyday examples, comparison tables and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and exam-focused practice.

question: A student listed four physical quantities measured during a laboratory experiment: density, momentum, work and temperature. Which pair consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question tests your ability to distinguish scalar quantities from vector quantities by identifying the pair that does not require a direction for complete description.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with measurable quantities known as physical quantities. These quantities are grouped into two major categories: scalar quantities and vector quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a physical quantity?' A physical quantity is any property that can be measured and expressed using a number together with a unit. Examples include mass, length, speed, force and temperature.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?' A scalar quantity is a quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or numerical value) and its unit only. It does not require a direction. For example, saying that the temperature of water is 40 °C completely describes its temperature.

Another important question is, 'What is a vector quantity?' A vector quantity is one that requires both magnitude and direction before it is fully described. For example, saying that a force is 20 N is incomplete unless its direction is also stated, such as 20 N eastward.

Let us now examine each quantity mentioned in the question.

Density is the mass per unit volume of a substance. It is calculated using:

Density = Mass ÷ Volume

Although density is obtained from other quantities, it has only magnitude and unit (kg m⁻³ or g cm⁻³). Therefore, density is a scalar quantity.

Momentum is defined as the product of mass and velocity.

Momentum = Mass × Velocity

Since velocity is a vector quantity, momentum also has direction. Therefore, momentum is a vector quantity.

Work is the energy transferred when a force causes displacement in its own direction.

Work = Force × Distance moved in the direction of the force

Although force is a vector, work itself has no direction. It is measured only by its magnitude in joules (J). Therefore, work is a scalar quantity.

Temperature tells how hot or cold a body is. It is measured in kelvin (K) or degree Celsius (°C). Temperature has magnitude only and no direction, so it is a scalar quantity.
steps: Step 1: List each physical quantity given in the question: density, momentum, work and temperature.

Step 2: Identify whether each quantity requires direction.

Density → No direction → Scalar.
Momentum → Has direction because it depends on velocity → Vector.
Work → No direction → Scalar.
Temperature → No direction → Scalar.

Step 3: Compare the options carefully.

Option A contains momentum, which is a vector.
Option B contains momentum.
Option C contains density and temperature, both of which are scalars.
Option D also contains momentum.
Step 4: Therefore, the only pair consisting entirely of scalar quantities is Density and Temperature.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (Density and momentum) is incorrect because momentum is a vector quantity. Although density is a scalar, the presence of one vector makes the pair incorrect.
Option B (Momentum and work) is incorrect because momentum is a vector. Work is a scalar, so the pair is mixed rather than entirely scalar.

Option D (Work and momentum) is incorrect for the same reason. Work is scalar but momentum is vector. JAMB often includes momentum in options because many candidates remember its formula but forget that velocity gives it direction.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests your understanding by mixing familiar quantities rather than asking for direct definitions. Learn common scalar quantities such as mass, time, temperature, density, pressure, energy, power, work, distance and speed. Also memorize common vector quantities such as displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum and weight. A useful memory aid is to always ask yourself: 'Would I need to specify a direction before this quantity is completely described?' If the answer is yes, it is a vector; otherwise, it is a scalar.
explanation.final_answer: C. Density and temperature
explanation.simple_explanation: Think about giving information to another person. If you say the temperature is 32 °C, nothing more is needed. If you say the density of a liquid is 1000 kg m⁻³, that is also complete. However, if you say an object has a momentum of 10 kg m s⁻¹, someone will immediately ask, 'In which direction?' Because momentum needs direction while density and temperature do not, density and temperature are the correct pair of scalar quantities.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalars to study how to distinguish scalar and vector quantities using definitions, memory techniques, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more beginner-friendly lessons and exam-focused practice.

question:
A student listed the following physical quantities measured during a laboratory practical:

I. Temperature of boiling water
II. Electric current flowing through a resistor
III. Pressure of a gas in a sealed container
IV. Force applied to compress a spring

Which combination consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question tests your ability to distinguish scalar quantities from vector quantities by identifying which listed physical quantities require only magnitude for complete description and which require both magnitude and direction.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, we must understand two important ideas: scalar quantities and vector quantities.

A physical quantity is any measurable property in Physics. Examples include length, mass, force, temperature and speed.

A beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?' Magnitude simply means the numerical size or amount of a quantity together with its unit. For example, 40 kg, 25°C and 5 A each have magnitudes.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is direction?' Direction tells us where a quantity acts or points. Examples include north, south, east, west, upward, downward, left and right.

A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude alone. Once you know its numerical value and unit, you have complete information.

Examples of scalar quantities include:

• Mass
• Time
• Temperature
• Distance
• Speed
• Energy
• Work
• Power
• Electric current
• Pressure
• Density
• Volume

A vector quantity is a physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction for complete description.

Examples include:

• Force
• Velocity
• Displacement
• Acceleration
• Momentum
• Weight

Notice that some quantities may seem similar but belong to different groups. For example:

• Distance is a scalar, while displacement is a vector.
• Speed is a scalar, while velocity is a vector.

For JAMB examinations, many questions test whether you can correctly classify quantities rather than merely memorize definitions.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Quantity I.

Temperature describes how hot or cold a body is. It has magnitude (for example, 100°C) but no direction. Therefore, temperature is a scalar quantity.

Step 2: Examine Quantity II.

Electric current measures the rate at which electric charge flows. Although electric current has a conventional direction in circuit analysis, it is treated in introductory Physics and JAMB as a scalar quantity because it is completely specified by its magnitude in amperes (A). Therefore, it is classified as a scalar.

Step 3: Examine Quantity III.

Pressure is defined as the normal force acting per unit area. Although force is a vector, pressure itself has no direction assigned to it as a physical quantity. It is measured simply by its magnitude in pascals (Pa). Therefore, pressure is a scalar quantity.

Step 4: Examine Quantity IV.

Force always acts in a particular direction. For example, a force may act upward, downward, eastward or westward. Without its direction, the description of a force is incomplete. Therefore, force is a vector quantity.

Step 5: Identify the quantities that are all scalars.

Scalars: I, II and III.

Vector: IV.

Hence, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (I, III and IV only) is incorrect because force (IV) is a vector quantity. Its direction must always be specified.
Option C (II, III and IV only) is incorrect because it also includes force, which is not a scalar.
Option D (I and IV only) is incorrect because it wrongly includes force as a scalar and also omits electric current and pressure, both of which are scalars.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently mixes familiar quantities to test whether candidates truly understand the difference between scalars and vectors. Do not classify a quantity based on everyday intuition. Instead, ask yourself one question:

'Is knowing only the numerical value enough, or must I also know the direction?'

If direction is essential, the quantity is a vector. If direction is unnecessary, it is a scalar. Pay particular attention to commonly tested pairs such as speed versus velocity and distance versus displacement.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and III only
explanation.simple_explanation: Temperature, electric current and pressure only need numerical values and units to describe them completely, so they are scalar quantities. Force is different because you must also know the direction in which it acts. Since only the first three are scalars, Option A is correct.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more beginner-friendly, exam-focused lessons.

question: A student records the following physical quantities during a physics practical: (i) the temperature of boiling water, (ii) the force used to pull a trolley eastward, (iii) the mass of the trolley, and (iv) the time taken for the trolley to travel between two points. Which combination consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to distinguish between scalar and vector quantities by identifying which of the listed physical quantities have magnitude only and which require both magnitude and direction.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, it is important to understand the meaning of a physical quantity.

A physical quantity is any measurable property of an object or event. Examples include length, mass, force, temperature and time.

Physical quantities are divided into two major groups:

1. Scalar quantities
2. Vector quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?'

A scalar quantity is a quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or numerical value) alone. It does not require any direction.

For example:
• A mass of 8 kg tells us everything we need to know about the object's mass.
• A temperature of 100°C completely describes the temperature.
• A time interval of 25 s is complete without mentioning any direction.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?'

Magnitude simply means the amount or numerical size of a quantity together with its unit. For example, 50 m, 7 kg and 18 s each have a magnitude.

Now consider vector quantities.

A vector quantity cannot be completely described by magnitude alone. It must also include direction.

For example:
• A force of 20 N is incomplete.
• A force of 20 N due east is complete because both the size (20 N) and the direction (east) have been stated.

Some common scalar quantities are:
• Mass
• Time
• Temperature
• Distance
• Speed
• Energy
• Work
• Power
• Volume
• Density

Some common vector quantities are:
• Force
• Velocity
• Displacement
• Acceleration
• Momentum
• Weight

One useful JAMB memory trick is:

If removing the direction changes the meaning of the quantity, it is most likely a vector. If direction is unnecessary, it is a scalar.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first quantity.

Temperature measures how hot or cold an object is. It has magnitude only and no direction. Therefore, temperature is a scalar quantity.

Step 2: Examine the second quantity.

Force is a push or pull acting on an object. A force is only fully described when both its magnitude and direction are given. In this question, the force acts eastward, showing that direction is important. Therefore, force is a vector quantity.

Step 3: Examine the third quantity.

Mass measures the amount of matter in an object. Whether the object is facing north, south, east or west does not change its mass. Therefore, mass is a scalar quantity.

Step 4: Examine the fourth quantity.

Time measures the duration of an event. Time does not have direction. Therefore, time is a scalar quantity.

Step 5: Identify the quantities that are all scalars.

Temperature ✓
Mass ✓
Time ✓
Force ✗ (vector)

Therefore, the correct combination is Temperature, Mass and Time.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (Force, temperature and time) is incorrect because force is a vector quantity. Although temperature and time are scalars, the presence of force means the entire combination is not made up exclusively of scalars.

Option C (Mass, force and temperature) is incorrect because force requires both magnitude and direction. Many candidates mistakenly classify force as a scalar simply because it is measured in newtons (N). The unit of a quantity does not determine whether it is scalar or vector.
Option D (Force, mass and time) is incorrect because force is a vector. Mass and time are scalars, but all the quantities in the option must be scalars for the option to be correct.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests your understanding by mixing scalar and vector quantities in the same list. Instead of memorising long lists, ask yourself one question: 'Does this quantity need a direction to describe it completely?' If the answer is yes, it is a vector. If the answer is no, it is a scalar. Remember that force, velocity, displacement, momentum and acceleration are common vectors, while mass, time, temperature, speed, energy and distance are common scalars.
explanation.final_answer: A. Temperature, mass and time
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think about whether you need to say where the quantity is pointing.

Mass is simply measured in kilograms.
Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius or kelvin.
Time is measured in seconds.

None of these needs a direction.

However, force is different. Saying '20 N' is incomplete because someone will naturally ask, 'In which direction?' Since force needs a direction, it is a vector, not a scalar.

Therefore, the only option containing only scalar quantities is Temperature, Mass and Time.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalars to learn how to distinguish scalar quantities from vector quantities, understand why certain physical quantities require direction, and practise more JAMB-style classification questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples and exam-focused practice.

question: A laboratory technician records the following quantities during an experiment: the mass of a metal block is 2.5 kg, the force applied to it is 15 N due east, the time taken for the motion is 8 s, and its displacement is 12 m due east. Which pair consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify the two quantities that are scalars from a list containing both scalar and vector quantities. To answer correctly, you must know the difference between scalar and vector quantities rather than simply memorizing examples.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with measurable quantities known as physical quantities. These quantities are grouped into two major classes: scalars and vectors.

A complete beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?' A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or numerical value) together with its unit. It does not require a direction to describe it fully.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?' Magnitude simply means the amount or numerical value of a quantity. For example, saying an object has a mass of 2.5 kg completely describes its mass. No direction such as north, south, east or west is needed.

A vector quantity, on the other hand, requires both magnitude and direction before it is completely described. For example, saying a force is 15 N is incomplete because force acts in a particular direction. Saying '15 N due east' gives both its magnitude (15 N) and its direction (due east), making the description complete.

Some common scalar quantities include mass, time, distance, speed, temperature, energy, work, power, volume and density. Common vector quantities include force, displacement, velocity, acceleration, momentum and weight.

A useful way to remember this is to ask yourself: 'If I remove the direction, is the quantity still fully described?' If the answer is yes, it is likely a scalar. If the answer is no, it is a vector.
steps: Step 1: Examine each quantity listed in the question.
Step 2: Mass = 2.5 kg. Mass only tells how much matter an object contains. It has magnitude only and no direction. Therefore, mass is a scalar quantity.
Step 3: Force = 15 N due east. The phrase 'due east' shows that direction is essential. Therefore, force is a vector quantity.
Step 4: Time = 8 s. Time measures duration. It has magnitude only and does not point in any direction. Therefore, time is a scalar quantity.
Step 5: Displacement = 12 m due east. Displacement describes change in position and requires direction. Therefore, displacement is a vector quantity.
Step 6: The only pair made up entirely of scalar quantities is mass and time.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (Mass and time) is correct because both quantities require only magnitude and units for complete description.
Option B (Force and displacement) is incorrect because both are vector quantities. Each requires both magnitude and direction.
Option C (Mass and displacement) is incorrect because although mass is a scalar, displacement is a vector. The pair is mixed.
Option D (Time and force) is incorrect because time is a scalar while force is a vector. Again, the pair is mixed.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB often mixes scalar and vector quantities in the same list to test whether candidates truly understand their differences. Do not rely on memorization alone. Ask yourself whether the quantity needs a direction to be completely described. If direction is essential, it is a vector. If direction is unnecessary, it is a scalar.
explanation.final_answer: A. Mass and time
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of a scalar as a quantity that answers only 'How much?' For example, '8 seconds' or '2.5 kilograms' is already complete. A vector answers both 'How much?' and 'In what direction?' For example, '15 N due east' or '12 m due east.' Since mass and time do not need directions, they are scalars.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalars to learn how to distinguish scalar and vector quantities, understand common examination tricks, and practise more JAMB-style questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons, worked examples and additional practice.

question: A laboratory technician records the following quantities during an experiment: acceleration due to gravity, electric charge, momentum and force. Which of these quantities can be completely specified without stating a direction?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify the quantity that is fully described by its magnitude alone and therefore does not require a direction for complete description.
explanation.principle:
Before answering this question, let us understand two important ideas.

A physical quantity is any measurable property in Physics. Examples include mass, time, force, speed and electric charge.

A beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?' Magnitude simply means the numerical size or amount of a quantity together with its unit. For example, 5 kg, 20 s and 12 C all have magnitudes.

Another beginner may ask, 'What is direction?' Direction tells us where a quantity acts or points. Examples include north, south, east, west, upward, downward or an angle measured from a reference direction.

A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that has magnitude only. Once its numerical value and unit are given, the quantity has been completely described.

A vector quantity is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Giving only its numerical value is not enough because its direction also affects its meaning.

For example, a force of 20 N acting east is different from a force of 20 N acting west, even though the magnitudes are the same.

Electric charge is measured in coulomb (C). Whether a body carries +5 C or -5 C indicates the type of charge (positive or negative), but it is not a direction in space. Therefore, electric charge is classified as a scalar quantity.

Acceleration, momentum and force are all vector quantities because each requires both magnitude and direction for complete specification.
steps: Step 1: Examine each option carefully instead of looking for familiar words.
Step 2: Ask whether the quantity needs a direction before it is completely described.
Step 3: Acceleration due to gravity acts vertically downward. Therefore, it has direction and is a vector.
Step 4: Electric charge is completely described by its magnitude and sign (+ or −). It does not require a spatial direction. Therefore, it is a scalar.
Step 5: Momentum depends on the direction of motion. Two objects may have the same momentum magnitude but different directions. Hence, momentum is a vector.
Step 6: Force always acts in a particular direction. Therefore, force is also a vector.
Step 7: The only scalar quantity among the options is electric charge.
why_other_options_are_wrong: A. Acceleration due to gravity is incorrect because acceleration describes the rate of change of velocity, and velocity itself has direction. Near the Earth's surface, gravitational acceleration acts vertically downward.

C. Momentum is incorrect because momentum depends on the direction in which an object moves. Two bodies moving in opposite directions cannot have the same momentum vector even if their magnitudes are equal.
D. Force is incorrect because every force acts in a particular direction. Two equal forces acting in opposite directions produce different physical effects.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently mixes scalar and vector quantities in the same options. Do not memorise isolated examples only. Learn the defining rule: if a quantity needs both magnitude and direction, it is a vector; if magnitude alone completely describes it, it is a scalar. Also remember that a positive or negative sign on electric charge does not represent a direction.
explanation.final_answer: B. Electric charge
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of electric charge as the amount of charge an object has. Once you say, for example, '+4 C' or '-2 C', you have completely described the charge. You do not need to add 'north', 'south' or any other direction. However, force, momentum and acceleration always act in particular directions, so they are vectors.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Examples of Scalar and Vector Quantities for a beginner-friendly lesson on how to distinguish scalars from vectors, common JAMB traps, worked examples and additional practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more detailed explanations and exam-focused practice.

question: A student records the following physical quantities during a laboratory practical: (i) the mass of a metal block, (ii) the temperature of boiling water, (iii) the time taken for a pendulum to complete 20 oscillations, and (iv) the speed of a moving trolley. Which statement correctly describes these quantities?

options.A: Only (i), (ii) and (iii) are scalar quantities because speed requires direction.
options.B: All the quantities listed are scalar quantities because each is completely described by magnitude only.
options.C: Only (i) and (ii) are scalar quantities because time and speed are vectors.
options.D: Only (iv) is a scalar quantity because it can be measured while the others cannot.

answer_text: All the quantities listed are scalar quantities because each is completely described by magnitude only.

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify whether mass, temperature, time and speed are scalar or vector quantities. Rather than memorizing individual examples, you must understand what makes a physical quantity a scalar.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with quantities that can be measured. Such measurable quantities are called physical quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a physical quantity?' A physical quantity is any property that can be measured and expressed using a number together with a unit. For example, 5 kg, 12 m, 30 s and 100 °C are all physical quantities because they contain both a numerical value and a unit.

Physical quantities are classified into two major groups:

1. Scalar quantities.
2. Vector quantities.

A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or numerical value) together with its unit. It does not require any direction for its description.

A beginner may ask, 'What is magnitude?' Magnitude simply means the amount or size of a quantity. For example, saying a bag has a mass of 8 kg completely describes its mass. There is no need to state whether the bag is north, south, east or west.

A vector quantity, on the other hand, requires both magnitude and direction before it is completely described. For example, saying a car moves at 20 m s⁻¹ is incomplete if the quantity is velocity. You must also specify a direction such as east or west.

Now examine each quantity carefully:

• Mass measures the amount of matter in an object. It only needs a value and unit (for example, 4 kg). Therefore, it is a scalar.

• Temperature indicates how hot or cold a body is. A reading such as 80 °C fully describes the temperature. No direction is required. Therefore, temperature is a scalar.

• Time measures duration or the interval between two events. Saying an experiment lasted 15 s completely describes the time. Time has magnitude only, so it is a scalar.

• Speed tells how fast an object moves. A value such as 12 m s⁻¹ is sufficient. Unlike velocity, speed does not include direction. Therefore, speed is also a scalar.

Students commonly confuse speed with velocity. Remember that speed tells 'how fast,' whereas velocity tells 'how fast and in what direction.'
steps: Step 1: Recall the definition of a scalar quantity. A scalar has magnitude only and does not require direction.
Step 2: Examine mass. It is expressed only by a numerical value and unit (kg). Therefore, it is a scalar.
Step 3: Examine temperature. It is expressed using units such as °C or K and requires no direction. Therefore, it is a scalar.
Step 4: Examine time. Time is measured in seconds, minutes or hours and has no direction. Therefore, it is a scalar.
Step 5: Examine speed. Speed tells only how fast an object moves. Since no direction is included, speed is also a scalar.
Step 6: Since all four quantities are scalars, the correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it assumes speed requires direction. This is a common misconception. It is velocity—not speed—that requires direction.
Option C is incorrect because time is never a vector quantity. Time has magnitude only and is therefore a scalar. Speed is also a scalar.
Option D is incorrect because all the listed quantities are measurable physical quantities. The statement wrongly suggests that only speed qualifies as a scalar.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests your ability to distinguish between closely related quantities such as speed and velocity, or distance and displacement. Whenever you see a list of physical quantities, ask yourself one simple question: 'Does this quantity require a direction before it is completely described?' If the answer is 'No,' it is a scalar. If the answer is 'Yes,' it is a vector.
explanation.final_answer: B. All the quantities listed are scalar quantities because each is completely described by magnitude only.
explanation.simple_explanation: A scalar quantity only needs a number and a unit. Mass, temperature, time and speed can all be fully described without mentioning any direction. For example, saying '5 kg', '100 °C', '20 s' or '8 m s⁻¹' is complete. Since none of them requires a direction like north, south, east or west, they are all scalar quantities.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more beginner-friendly lessons and exam-focused explanations.

question:
A student listed four physical quantities measured during a laboratory practical as follows:

I. Mass of a metal block
II. Temperature of boiling water
III. Electric current in a circuit
IV. Weight of the metal block

Which option correctly identifies the quantities that are scalars?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify which quantities are scalars from a mixed list containing both scalar and vector quantities. To answer correctly, you must understand what makes a quantity a scalar and distinguish it from a vector.
explanation.principle:
Physics deals with quantities that describe measurable properties of objects and events. These measurable properties are called physical quantities.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a physical quantity?' A physical quantity is any property that can be measured and expressed using a number and a unit. For example, 5 kg, 30°C and 12 m are physical quantities because they have numerical values and units.

Physical quantities are grouped into two broad classes:

1. Scalar quantities
A scalar quantity is completely described by its magnitude (size or amount) only. It does not require a direction for its meaning. If you say a bag has a mass of 8 kg, that information is complete because no direction is needed.

Examples of scalar quantities include mass, time, temperature, distance, speed, energy, work, power, electric current, volume, density, pressure and charge.

2. Vector quantities
A vector quantity requires both magnitude and direction before it is completely described. For example, saying an object experiences a force of 20 N is incomplete unless the direction is also stated, such as '20 N eastward.' Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration, momentum, force and weight.

A beginner may now ask, 'Why is weight a vector while mass is a scalar?' Mass measures the amount of matter contained in an object and has no direction. Weight, however, is the gravitational force acting on that mass. Since every force acts in a particular direction, weight is always directed towards the centre of the Earth. Therefore, weight is a vector quantity.

Another beginner may ask, 'Is electric current really a scalar?' Yes. Although electric current flows through a conductor, the quantity called current is treated as a scalar in elementary Physics because it is completely specified by its magnitude. The direction of flow is handled separately in circuit analysis and does not make current a vector quantity in JAMB-level Physics.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Quantity I (Mass).

Mass measures the amount of matter in a body. It has magnitude only and no direction.

Therefore, Mass is a scalar.

Step 2: Examine Quantity II (Temperature).

Temperature indicates how hot or cold a body is. It only tells the degree of hotness or coldness and has no direction.

Therefore, Temperature is a scalar.

Step 3: Examine Quantity III (Electric current).

Electric current measures the rate at which electric charge flows through a conductor. At JAMB level, it is classified as a scalar quantity because only its magnitude is required to specify it.

Therefore, Electric current is a scalar.

Step 4: Examine Quantity IV (Weight).

Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object. Since every force has both magnitude and direction, weight is a vector.

Therefore, Weight is NOT a scalar.

Step 5: Collect all the scalar quantities.

The scalars are I, II and III.

Hence, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (I, II and IV only) is incorrect because it wrongly classifies weight as a scalar. Weight is a force and therefore a vector quantity.
Option C (II, III and IV only) is incorrect because it excludes mass, which is one of the most common examples of a scalar quantity. It also incorrectly includes weight.
Option D (I and IV only) is incorrect because although mass is a scalar, weight is a vector. It also ignores temperature and electric current, both of which are scalars.
explanation.exam_tip: JAMB frequently tests your ability to distinguish between closely related quantities such as mass and weight, speed and velocity, or distance and displacement. Whenever you encounter such pairs, ask yourself one important question: 'Does this quantity require a direction before it is completely described?' If the answer is yes, it is a vector. If not, it is a scalar. Remember that mass is scalar, but weight is vector because weight is a force.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and III only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of a scalar as a quantity that only tells 'how much.' For example, a temperature of 100°C or a mass of 5 kg is complete without mentioning any direction.

A vector tells both 'how much' and 'which way.' Weight is a force pulling objects towards the Earth, so direction is important. Therefore, only mass, temperature and electric current are scalars in this list.

question: A student records the following physical quantities during a laboratory experiment: (i) the mass of a metal block, (ii) the temperature of the water used, (iii) the displacement of the block from its initial position, and (iv) the time taken for the experiment. Which combination consists entirely of scalar quantities?

explanation.question_restate: The question asks you to identify which of the listed physical quantities are scalar quantities. To answer correctly, you must know the difference between scalar and vector quantities rather than simply memorizing examples.
explanation.principle:
Before solving this question, let us first understand what a physical quantity is.

A physical quantity is any measurable property of an object or event. Examples include length, mass, speed, force and temperature. Every physical quantity consists of a numerical value and a unit.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a scalar quantity?'

A scalar quantity is a physical quantity that is completely described by its magnitude (size or amount) alone. It does not require a direction to have complete meaning.

Magnitude simply means how much or how big the quantity is. For example, saying that the mass of an object is 5 kg completely describes its mass. There is no need to specify a direction.

Examples of scalar quantities include mass, time, temperature, distance, speed, energy, power, density, pressure and volume.

A beginner may then ask, 'What is a vector quantity?'

A vector quantity is a physical quantity that requires both magnitude and direction before it is completely described. Without the direction, the information is incomplete.

For example, saying that an object moved 10 m is not enough if the quantity is displacement. We must also state the direction, such as 10 m east or 10 m north.

Examples of vector quantities include displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum and weight.

One common JAMB trick is to include both 'distance' and 'displacement' in the same question. Although the two words look similar, distance is a scalar because it only tells how much ground has been covered, while displacement is a vector because it describes both the change in position and the direction of that change.
steps:
Step 1: Examine 'mass of a metal block'.

Mass measures the amount of matter contained in an object. It only requires a numerical value and the unit kilogram (kg). No direction is involved. Therefore, mass is a scalar quantity.

Step 2: Examine 'temperature of the water'.

Temperature indicates how hot or cold a substance is. It is measured in kelvin (K) or degrees Celsius (°C). Since temperature has magnitude only and no direction, it is also a scalar quantity.

Step 3: Examine 'displacement of the block'.

Displacement describes the change in position of an object from its starting point to its final point. It requires both magnitude and direction. For example, '4 m east' is meaningful, whereas '4 m' alone is incomplete. Therefore, displacement is a vector quantity and must not appear in a group consisting entirely of scalars.

Step 4: Examine 'time taken for the experiment'.

Time measures the duration of an event. Whether the experiment lasted 5 s or 20 s, no direction is required. Hence, time is a scalar quantity.

Step 5: Identify the option containing only scalar quantities.

Mass ✓
Temperature ✓
Time ✓
Displacement ✗ (vector)

Therefore, the correct combination is Mass, Temperature and Time.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A: This option includes displacement. Displacement is not a scalar because it requires both magnitude and direction. Including one vector quantity makes the entire combination incorrect.

Option C: Although temperature and time are scalars, displacement is again included. Many candidates confuse displacement with distance because both are measured in metres. Remember that distance is scalar, while displacement is vector.
Option D: Mass and time are scalars, but displacement remains a vector quantity. The presence of displacement makes this option incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests your ability to distinguish between similar physical quantities. Pay special attention to the following pairs:

• Distance (scalar) versus Displacement (vector).
• Speed (scalar) versus Velocity (vector).
• Mass (scalar) versus Weight (vector).

Whenever you see words such as 'towards', 'north', 'south', 'east', 'west', 'upward' or 'downward', ask yourself whether direction is an essential part of the quantity. If direction is required for the quantity to make complete sense, then it is a vector.
explanation.final_answer: B. Mass, temperature and time only.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of a scalar quantity as something that answers only the question 'How much?'

Mass tells how much matter an object contains.
Temperature tells how hot or cold something is.
Time tells how long an event lasts.

None of these needs a direction.

Displacement is different. If someone says, 'Walk 20 m,' you still need to ask, 'In which direction?' Because direction is necessary, displacement is a vector, not a scalar. Therefore, the only group made up entirely of scalar quantities is mass, temperature and time.
