Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and practice.

question:
Engineers are planning to lift a large concrete slab at a construction site using two cranes. The lifting cables will pull simultaneously on the slab from different directions.

Which method is most appropriate for determining the single force that can replace the two pulling forces acting at the same time?

options.D: Simple arithmetic addition of the force magnitudes

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the appropriate method for combining two vectors that act simultaneously from the same point in a practical engineering situation.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors is used when two vectors act simultaneously at a common point.

A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, displacement, velocity and acceleration.

When two forces act together on the same object at the same time, they produce a combined effect known as the resultant force.

Instead of considering the two forces separately, they can be replaced by one equivalent force called the resultant.

The parallelogram law provides a graphical method for finding this resultant. Imagine drawing the two vectors from the same starting point as adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal drawn from the common starting point represents the resultant vector in both magnitude and direction.

This method is especially suitable when the vectors are concurrent, meaning they act simultaneously through the same point.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what is happening in the question.

Two cranes are pulling the same concrete slab at the same time. Therefore, the two forces are acting simultaneously on one object.

Step 2: Decide which vector addition method matches this situation.

Whenever two vectors originate from the same point and act simultaneously, the parallelogram law is the appropriate method for combining them.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option A correctly identifies the parallelogram law.

Option B refers to the triangle law, which is commonly used when vectors are added successively using the head-to-tail method.

Option C is incorrect because forces are vectors, not scalars.

Option D is incorrect because simply adding magnitudes ignores the directions of the forces, which strongly influence the resultant.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the triangle law is primarily used for successive vector addition rather than two concurrent vectors acting simultaneously from the same point.
Option C is incorrect because forces possess both magnitude and direction. They cannot be treated as scalar quantities.
Option D is incorrect because adding only the magnitudes ignores the angle between the forces. The resultant depends on both magnitude and direction.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB Physics, pay attention to expressions such as 'acting simultaneously', 'acting at a point', or 'concurrent forces'. These are strong clues that the parallelogram law of vectors is the appropriate method. Whenever direction matters, avoid treating vectors like ordinary numbers.
explanation.final_answer: A. Parallelogram law of vectors
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two people pushing the same heavy object from different directions at exactly the same time. Instead of describing each push separately, Physics allows us to replace them with one imaginary push that has the same overall effect. When the pushes act together from the same point, the parallelogram law is the correct method for finding that single equivalent push, called the resultant.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how concurrent vectors are combined, why the diagonal of the parallelogram represents the resultant, and how to solve JAMB-style application and calculation questions using this method.

question:
During the construction of a bridge, two supporting cables pull simultaneously on a steel beam. One cable exerts a force of 22 N while the other exerts a force of 34 N. The angle between the two forces is 60°.

Using the parallelogram law of vectors, what is the magnitude of the resultant force acting on the beam?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the magnitude of the resultant of two forces acting simultaneously at an angle by applying the parallelogram law of vectors.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that if two vectors acting simultaneously at a point are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then the diagonal passing through their common point represents the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add the two forces?'

The answer is that forces are vectors. A vector has both magnitude and direction. When two forces act at an angle, part of each force acts in different directions. Therefore, the angle between them must be considered when determining their combined effect.

For two vectors of magnitudes P and Q with an included angle θ, the magnitude of the resultant R is given by:

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cosθ)

where:

• P = magnitude of the first vector
• Q = magnitude of the second vector
• θ = angle between the vectors
• R = magnitude of the resultant vector
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

P = 22 N
Q = 34 N
θ = 60°

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cosθ)

Step 3: Substitute the values.

R = √(22² + 34² + 2 × 22 × 34 × cos60°)

Step 5: Add the terms.

R = √(484 + 1156 + 748)

R = √2388

Step 6: Calculate the square root.

√2388 ≈ 48.87 N

The closest option is 48 N.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (56 N) is obtained by simply adding the two forces (22 N + 34 N), ignoring the angle between them.
Option C (40 N) is a plausible error resulting from incorrect substitution into the formula or premature rounding.
Option D (28 N) may arise from incorrectly subtracting the forces or using the wrong trigonometric relationship.
common_misconceptions: Assuming vectors can always be added arithmetically.
Ignoring the included angle between the vectors.
Using Pythagoras' theorem even when the vectors are not at 90°.
Applying the cosine term incorrectly.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two forces act simultaneously at an angle, first identify whether the angle is given. If it is, the parallelogram law is usually the appropriate method for calculating the resultant. Remember that direct addition of magnitudes is correct only when the forces act in exactly the same direction.
explanation.final_answer: A. 48 N
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two workers pulling the same steel beam using ropes from different directions. Because they are not pulling in exactly the same direction, their forces do not simply add together. The parallelogram law combines both the sizes of the forces and the angle between them to determine the single force that has the same overall effect. In this case, the resultant force is approximately 48 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how to calculate resultants of concurrent vectors, understand the cosine formula, interpret vector diagrams and solve additional JAMB-style worked examples at the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
In a mechanical workshop, two technicians apply two forces simultaneously to loosen a tightly fitted machine component. One force acts towards the north-east while the other acts towards the north-west from the same point.

A parallelogram is constructed using the two force vectors as adjacent sides. Which part of the construction represents the resultant force acting on the component?

options.A: The diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the common point of application
options.B: Any one of the adjacent sides of the parallelogram
options.C: The side opposite the longer vector
options.D: The line joining the midpoints of opposite sides of the parallelogram

answer_text: The diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the common point of application

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to interpret a text-described vector diagram constructed using the parallelogram law. Rather than performing a calculation, you are expected to identify the line that represents the resultant of two simultaneous vectors.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors states that when two vectors act simultaneously at the same point and are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, their resultant is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the common point where both vectors originate.

A beginner may ask, 'What is a resultant vector?'

A resultant vector is the single vector that produces the same overall effect as two or more vectors acting together.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why is the diagonal used?'

When two vectors act at the same point, each contributes to the overall motion or force. The diagonal combines both the magnitudes and directions into one equivalent vector. This is why the diagonal accurately represents the combined effect.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the situation.

The two forces are applied simultaneously from the same point. This is exactly the condition under which the parallelogram law is used.

Step 2: Recall how the parallelogram is constructed.

Each force is represented by one adjacent side of the parallelogram starting from the same point of application.

Step 3: Locate the resultant.

After completing the parallelogram, the resultant is represented by the diagonal drawn from the common starting point to the opposite corner.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly identifies this diagonal as the resultant vector.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because an adjacent side represents one of the original vectors, not their combined effect.
Option C is incorrect because the side opposite a vector is simply a parallel copy used to complete the parallelogram. It does not represent the resultant.
Option D is incorrect because joining the midpoints of opposite sides has no role in determining the resultant in the parallelogram law.
explanation.common_misconception: Many students mistakenly believe that one of the sides of the parallelogram represents the resultant. In reality, the sides represent the original vectors, while the diagonal from the common point represents their combined effect.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question mentions two vectors acting simultaneously from the same point and refers to a parallelogram construction, immediately remember:

• Adjacent sides represent the original vectors.
• The diagonal from the common point represents the resultant.
• The opposite sides are only construction lines and do not represent the resultant.
explanation.final_answer: A. The diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the common point of application
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pushing a heavy machine from different directions at the same time. Instead of thinking about each push separately, Physics replaces both pushes with one imaginary push that has exactly the same effect.

In the parallelogram method, this single equivalent push is shown by the diagonal drawn from the point where both pushes begin. That diagonal is called the resultant vector.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how concurrent vectors are combined graphically, why the diagonal represents the resultant, how to interpret vector diagrams, and how to solve JAMB-style questions using the parallelogram method. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and concept reviews.

question:
During an industrial maintenance exercise, two technicians discussed methods of adding vectors.

Technician I stated that the triangle law is suitable when vectors are added successively by placing the head of one vector at the tail of the next.

Technician II stated that the parallelogram law is mainly used when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point.

Which of the following correctly compares the two methods?

options.A: Both Technician I and Technician II are correct.
options.B: Technician I is correct, but Technician II is incorrect.
options.C: Technician I is incorrect, but Technician II is correct.
options.D: Both Technician I and Technician II are incorrect.

answer_text: Both Technician I and Technician II are correct.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the triangle law and the parallelogram law of vector addition. It tests whether you understand the situations in which each method is most appropriately applied rather than requiring any numerical calculation.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction.

When two or more vectors are combined, the process is called vector addition, and the single vector that produces the same overall effect is known as the resultant vector.

There are different methods for adding vectors because vectors may be arranged differently.

The **triangle law of vectors** is used when vectors are considered one after another. The second vector is drawn from the head of the first vector, and the resultant is represented by the line joining the tail of the first vector to the head of the second.

The **parallelogram law of vectors** is used when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point. The two vectors are drawn from a common origin, a parallelogram is completed, and the diagonal through the common origin represents the resultant.

Although both methods determine the same resultant when applied correctly, they differ in how the vectors are arranged graphically.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Technician I's statement.

The triangle law places vectors head-to-tail and is commonly used to represent successive vector addition.

Therefore, Technician I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Technician II's statement.

The parallelogram law begins with two vectors acting from the same point simultaneously. The diagonal of the completed parallelogram gives the resultant.

Therefore, Technician II is also correct.

Step 3: Compare the two methods.

The triangle law and the parallelogram law are both valid methods of vector addition, but they differ in the graphical arrangement of the vectors.

Since both technicians described their respective methods correctly, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Technician II correctly described the parallelogram law as applying to two vectors acting simultaneously from the same point.
Option C is incorrect because Technician I correctly described the triangle law as a head-to-tail method for successive vector addition.
Option D is incorrect because both statements are scientifically correct and consistent with the principles of vector addition.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, you may be asked to distinguish between different graphical methods of vector addition.

Remember these key points:

• Triangle law: vectors are arranged head-to-tail.

• Parallelogram law: vectors start from the same point and the diagonal gives the resultant.

Do not confuse the arrangement of the vectors with the purpose of the methods. Both are used to determine the resultant vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. Both Technician I and Technician II are correct.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine moving a box.

If one worker pushes first and another continues from where the first stopped, the movement resembles the triangle law because the actions occur successively.

If two workers push the box at the same time from different directions, the situation resembles the parallelogram law because both forces act simultaneously from the same point.

Both methods can be used to find the combined effect of the vectors; they simply arrange the vectors differently.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn the differences between the triangle law and the parallelogram law, when each method should be used, how to construct them graphically, and how JAMB tests these concepts through conceptual and application-based questions.

question:
Two tow trucks pull a disabled vehicle simultaneously using forces of 36 N and 52 N. The ropes make an angle of 90° with each other.

Using the parallelogram law of vector addition, what is the magnitude of the resultant towing force acting on the vehicle?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the single equivalent force produced when two tow trucks pull a disabled vehicle simultaneously at right angles. The parallelogram law of vectors is used because both forces act concurrently from the same point.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that when two vectors act simultaneously at a point, they can be represented by the adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal drawn from their common starting point represents the resultant vector.

Whenever two forces act at an angle, the magnitude of the resultant is obtained using:

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

where:

• R = magnitude of the resultant vector
• P = first vector
• Q = second vector
• θ = included angle between the vectors

A beginner may ask, 'Why is the angle important?'

The angle determines how much the two vectors reinforce one another. Forces in the same direction produce the largest resultant, while forces in opposite directions reduce the resultant. When the angle is 90°, the vectors are perpendicular and the cosine term becomes zero.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

First force, P = 36 N

Second force, Q = 52 N

Included angle, θ = 90°

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

R = √(36² + 52² + 2 × 36 × 52 × cos 90°)

Step 4: Evaluate the cosine term.

cos 90° = 0

Therefore,

R = √(36² + 52²)

Step 5: Perform the calculations.

36² = 1296

52² = 2704

1296 + 2704 = 4000

R = √4000 ≈ 63.25 N

Step 6: Compare with the available options.

The calculated value is approximately 63 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (74 N) is incorrect. It may result from using an incorrect formula or adding the forces inappropriately.
Option C (88 N) is incorrect. This value is obtained by simply adding the two forces (36 N + 52 N), ignoring the 90° angle between them.
Option D (94 N) is incorrect. This overestimates the resultant and does not satisfy the parallelogram law for perpendicular vectors.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever two vectors act at right angles, remember that cos 90° = 0. This simplifies the parallelogram-law formula to:

R = √(P² + Q²)

Do not simply add the magnitudes unless the vectors act in exactly the same direction.
explanation.final_answer: A. 63 N
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two tow trucks pulling a vehicle from different directions at right angles. Because the pulls are not in the same direction, they cannot be added directly. Instead, they combine to produce a single diagonal pull called the resultant. Using the parallelogram law gives a resultant of about 63 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors for detailed lessons on concurrent forces, vector addition at different angles, graphical construction of parallelograms, and fully worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations and additional practice.

question:
A robotics team is combining two simultaneous force vectors acting on the arm of an automated machine using the parallelogram law.

After drawing the two vectors from the same point, one student constructs a four-sided figure but draws the resultant along one of the sides of the figure instead of across it.

Which mistake did the student make?

options.A: The resultant should be represented by the diagonal of the completed parallelogram drawn from the common point.
options.B: The resultant should always be the longer side of the parallelogram.
options.C: The two vectors should first be drawn head-to-tail before constructing the parallelogram.
options.D: The resultant should be obtained by adding the lengths of the two adjacent sides.

answer_text: The resultant should be represented by the diagonal of the completed parallelogram drawn from the common point.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests whether you can identify a common construction error when applying the parallelogram law of vector addition. Rather than performing calculations, you are expected to recognise the correct graphical representation of the resultant vector.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point, they can be represented by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram.

The single vector that has the same combined effect as the two original vectors is called the **resultant vector**.

A beginner may ask, 'Where is the resultant drawn?'

The resultant is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram that starts from the common point where the two vectors originate.

The sides of the parallelogram represent the original vectors and their translated copies, not the resultant.

Therefore, drawing the resultant along one of the sides is a construction error.
steps:
Step 1: Identify how the original vectors are drawn.

The question states that the two vectors begin from the same point. This is the correct starting point for applying the parallelogram law.

Step 2: Recall the graphical construction.

Complete the parallelogram by drawing lines parallel to each of the original vectors.

This creates a four-sided figure whose opposite sides are parallel and equal.

Step 3: Locate the resultant.

The resultant is not one of the sides.

It is the diagonal drawn from the common starting point to the opposite corner of the completed parallelogram.

Step 4: Identify the student's error.

Since the student drew the resultant along one of the sides instead of the diagonal, the graphical construction was incorrect.

Therefore, Option A correctly identifies the mistake.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the resultant is not chosen based on the longest side. Its magnitude and direction depend on both vectors and the included angle between them.
Option C is incorrect because drawing vectors head-to-tail is used for the triangle law of vector addition. The parallelogram law requires the vectors to start from the same point.
Option D is incorrect because vector addition cannot generally be performed by simply adding side lengths. The angle between the vectors must also be considered.
explanation.exam_tip:
When answering JAMB questions on the parallelogram law, remember this simple rule:

• Two vectors acting simultaneously start from the same point.
• Complete the parallelogram.
• The resultant is the diagonal drawn from the common origin.

A frequent examination trap is to label one of the sides as the resultant. Always check that the diagonal—not a side—is being used.
explanation.final_answer: A. The resultant should be represented by the diagonal of the completed parallelogram drawn from the common point.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pushing a robot arm in different directions at the same time.

The two pushes are shown by the two sides of a parallelogram.

To find their combined effect, you do not choose one of the sides. Instead, you draw a diagonal from where both pushes started.

That diagonal shows the resultant force.

Therefore, the student's mistake was using a side instead of the diagonal.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors for a detailed explanation of graphical vector addition, construction of parallelograms, identification of the resultant vector, common examination mistakes, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and additional practice.

question: A meteorologist observes that a weather balloon is simultaneously influenced by two wind currents blowing from different directions at the same time. To determine the balloon's overall direction of motion, which vector principle should be applied?

options.C: Scalar addition of the wind speeds
options.D: Resolution of one wind vector only

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the appropriate vector principle for combining two forces acting simultaneously on the same object. Instead of performing calculations, it assesses your understanding of when the parallelogram law should be used.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that when two vectors act simultaneously at a point and are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, their resultant is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from their common point of origin.

A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Wind velocity is a vector because it describes both how fast the wind blows and the direction in which it blows.

When more than one wind current acts on a weather balloon at the same time, the balloon responds to the combined effect of both vectors rather than either one individually. The single vector that represents this combined effect is called the resultant vector.

The parallelogram law is particularly suitable whenever two vectors act concurrently (simultaneously) on the same object from a common point.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the physical situation.

The weather balloon is acted upon by two wind currents at the same time.

Step 2: Recognise that each wind current has both magnitude and direction.

Therefore, each wind current is represented by a vector rather than a scalar.

Step 3: Determine the appropriate method.

Since the two vectors act simultaneously on the same object, the parallelogram law is the appropriate graphical method for obtaining their resultant.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly identifies the vector principle used.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the triangle law is primarily used by placing vectors head-to-tail rather than representing two concurrent vectors acting from the same point as adjacent sides of a parallelogram.
Option C is incorrect because wind velocity is a vector quantity. Simply adding the speeds ignores the directions of the wind currents, which are essential in vector addition.
Option D is incorrect because considering only one wind vector neglects the effect of the second wind current and cannot describe the balloon's actual motion.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question mentions two forces, velocities or other vectors acting simultaneously on the same object from a common point, think of the parallelogram law. Do not add their magnitudes directly unless they act in exactly the same direction.
explanation.final_answer: A. Parallelogram law of vector addition
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pushing a large advertising balloon from different directions at the same time. The balloon moves in a direction that is influenced by both pushes together.

The parallelogram law helps us combine the two pushes into one equivalent push called the resultant vector. This is why meteorologists can use it to predict the overall movement of a weather balloon affected by different wind currents.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn when the method is applicable, how to construct the parallelogram graphically, how to identify the resultant vector, and how the method differs from the triangle law. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples, and JAMB-focused practice questions.

question:
To improve stability while lifting a large steel container, two cables attached to a crane pull simultaneously on the load. One cable exerts a force of 42 N and the other exerts a force of 31 N. If the angle between the two forces is 45°, what is the magnitude of the resultant force on the container?

(Take \(\sqrt{2}=1.414\).)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the resultant of two concurrent forces acting at an angle by applying the parallelogram law of vectors.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point, they can be represented by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal drawn from their common point represents the resultant vector in both magnitude and direction.

Since force is a vector quantity, both its magnitude and direction must be considered. When two forces act at an angle, the resultant depends on the included angle between them.

The magnitude of the resultant is given by:

\[
R=\sqrt{P^{2}+Q^{2}+2PQ\cos\theta}
\]

where:

• \(P\) = first force
• \(Q\) = second force
• \(\theta\) = included angle
• \(R\) = resultant force
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

P = 42 N
Q = 31 N
θ = 45°

Since cos45° = √2/2 = 1.414/2 = 0.707.

Step 2: Substitute into the formula.

R² = 42² + 31² + 2(42)(31)(0.707)

Step 4: Find the square root.

R = √4565.356
R ≈ 67.57 N

Rounded to one decimal place,

R ≈ 67.6 N.
Step 5: Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (73.0 N) results from overestimating the resultant by treating the forces almost as if they acted in the same direction.
Option C (51.4 N) arises from an incorrect application of the formula or an arithmetic error during substitution.
Option D (11.0 N) is obtained by simply subtracting the magnitudes of the two forces and completely ignoring the angle between them.
explanation.exam_tip: For JAMB questions involving two concurrent forces acting at an angle, immediately think of the parallelogram law. Remember that the included angle plays a vital role. Only when two forces act in exactly the same direction can their magnitudes be added directly.
explanation.simple_explanation: Two crane cables pull the same load from different directions. Their combined effect is not found by ordinary addition because the pulls are not in the same direction. The parallelogram law combines both the magnitudes of the forces and the angle between them. Using the given values gives a resultant force of approximately 67.6 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors for a detailed explanation of concurrent vectors, derivation of the parallelogram law formula, fully worked examples, common examination mistakes, and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
During a Physics lesson on the parallelogram law of vectors, a teacher asked four students to describe the method. They made the following statements:

Student I: 'The parallelogram law is used to determine the resultant of two vectors acting simultaneously from the same point.'

Student II: 'The resultant vector is represented by the diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from the common point of the two vectors.'

Student III: 'The magnitude of the resultant is always equal to the sum of the magnitudes of the two vectors.'

Student IV: 'The parallelogram law can be applied to vector quantities other than forces, provided the vectors act from a common point.'

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of the principles of the parallelogram law of vectors by asking you to identify which statements correctly describe the law and its applications.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors is a graphical method used to combine two vectors that act simultaneously from the same point. It states that if the two vectors are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram drawn from a common point, then the diagonal passing through that common point represents the resultant vector in both magnitude and direction.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is it called the parallelogram law?'

It is because the construction requires a parallelogram to be drawn using the two vectors as adjacent sides.

Another beginner may ask, 'Does this method apply only to forces?'

No. It applies to any vector quantities, such as velocity, displacement, acceleration and momentum, provided two vectors originate from the same point and are being combined.

One common misconception is believing that the resultant is always equal to the arithmetic sum of the magnitudes. This is true only when both vectors act in exactly the same direction. When the vectors are inclined to one another, the included angle affects the magnitude of the resultant.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Student I's statement.

This statement is correct.

The parallelogram law is specifically used to obtain the resultant of two vectors acting simultaneously from a common point.

Step 2: Examine Student II's statement.

This statement is correct.

After constructing the parallelogram, the diagonal drawn from the common point represents the resultant vector.

Step 3: Examine Student III's statement.

This statement is incorrect.

The resultant is not always the arithmetic sum of the magnitudes. Its value depends on both the magnitudes of the vectors and the angle between them.

Step 4: Examine Student IV's statement.

This statement is correct.

The parallelogram law is a general method for adding vectors and is not restricted to forces alone.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Students I, II and IV are correct, while Student III is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Student III, whose statement ignores the effect of the included angle between vectors.
Option C is incorrect because although Student II is correct, Student III is not. It also omits Students I and IV, which are correct.
Option D is incorrect because it also includes Student III, making the combination incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks conceptual questions on the parallelogram law, remember these key facts:

• The vectors must originate from the same point.

• The two vectors form the adjacent sides of a parallelogram.

• The diagonal from the common point gives the resultant.

• The resultant depends on both magnitude and the included angle.

Be cautious of statements containing the word 'always.' In vector problems, such statements are often incorrect unless special conditions are specified.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pushing a heavy machine at the same time from the same position but in different directions.

Instead of considering each push separately, the parallelogram law helps us find one imaginary push that produces the same overall effect.

The diagonal of the parallelogram represents that single combined push, called the resultant.

Because the direction of each push matters, you cannot always add their magnitudes directly. That is why Student III is wrong, while Students I, II and IV are correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how the law is constructed, why the diagonal represents the resultant, when the method should be used, how it differs from the triangle law, and how JAMB tests conceptual and calculation-based questions on this topic.

question:
A warehouse uses two forklifts to pull a heavy machine simultaneously from the same point in different directions. Four technicians describe different graphical constructions for obtaining the single force that represents the combined effect of the two pulls.

Technician I: Place the two force vectors tail-to-tail, complete a parallelogram using the vectors as adjacent sides, and draw the diagonal from their common starting point.

Technician II: Place the vectors head-to-head and join their free ends with a straight line.

Technician III: Draw the two vectors separately without a common starting point, then measure the distance between their tips.

Technician IV: Add the magnitudes of the two forces directly and represent the answer as the resultant regardless of the angle between them.

Which technician has correctly represented the graphical construction required by the parallelogram law?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to recognise the correct graphical construction used in the parallelogram law of vector addition. Rather than performing a calculation, you are expected to identify the proper procedure for obtaining the resultant of two vectors acting simultaneously.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law is a graphical method used to add two vectors that act simultaneously from the same point.

A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction.

Examples include force, displacement, velocity, acceleration and momentum.

When two vectors act at the same point, they can be represented as two adjacent sides of a parallelogram.

The diagonal drawn from the common starting point (tail) to the opposite corner of the parallelogram represents the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is the diagonal used?'

The diagonal combines the effects of both vectors into one equivalent vector that produces exactly the same overall effect as the two original vectors acting together.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Technician I's description.

The two vectors are drawn from the same starting point, a parallelogram is completed, and the diagonal from the common origin is taken as the resultant.

This is the correct graphical construction for the parallelogram law.

Step 2: Examine Technician II's description.

Placing vectors head-to-head does not produce the standard parallelogram construction for vector addition. The resultant is not obtained by simply joining their free ends.

Therefore, Technician II is incorrect.

Step 3: Examine Technician III's description.

Drawing vectors without a common origin and measuring the distance between their tips does not represent vector addition.

Therefore, Technician III is incorrect.

Step 4: Examine Technician IV's description.

Simply adding the magnitudes ignores the directions of the vectors. This method is valid only when both vectors act in exactly the same direction.

Since the vectors act in different directions, this procedure is incorrect.

Step 5: Select the correct technician.

Only Technician I correctly applies the parallelogram law.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because placing vectors head-to-head is not the graphical procedure used in the parallelogram law.
Option C is incorrect because vectors must originate from the same point before constructing the parallelogram. Measuring the distance between vector tips does not produce the resultant.
Option D is incorrect because vector addition depends on both magnitude and direction. Adding magnitudes directly ignores the included angle between the vectors.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, questions on the parallelogram law are not always numerical. You should also know the correct graphical construction.

Remember these key points:

• The vectors must start from the same point.
• The vectors form two adjacent sides of a parallelogram.
• The resultant is the diagonal drawn from the common starting point.
• Direction is just as important as magnitude when adding vectors.
explanation.final_answer: A. Technician I only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pulling a heavy crate at the same time from the same point but in different directions.

To find one force that has the same overall effect as both pulls, draw the two force vectors from the same starting point, complete a parallelogram, and draw the diagonal beginning from that common point.

That diagonal is called the resultant vector.

Only Technician I described this correctly.

Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
During an emergency rescue operation, two rescue teams pull a trapped vehicle simultaneously using separate ropes. One team exerts a force of 65 N while the other exerts a force of 48 N. The angle between the two ropes is 120°.

Using the parallelogram law of vectors, what is the magnitude of the resultant pulling force on the vehicle?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the single resultant force produced when two rescue teams pull simultaneously in different directions. Since the two forces act at an angle, the parallelogram law of vectors is used.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Force is a vector quantity because it describes not only how large a push or pull is but also the direction in which it acts.

When two forces act simultaneously at the same point and are inclined to each other, they can be replaced by one equivalent force called the **resultant**. The parallelogram law states that if the two forces are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then the diagonal drawn from their common point represents the resultant.

For calculations, the magnitude of the resultant is obtained using:

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

where:

• R = resultant force
• P and Q = magnitudes of the two forces
• θ = included angle between the forces

The value of the cosine depends on the angle. For 120°, cos120° = -0.5. The negative value indicates that the forces oppose each other to some extent, making the resultant smaller than the arithmetic sum of the forces.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

P = 65 N
Q = 48 N
θ = 120°

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

R = √(65² + 48² + 2 × 65 × 48 × cos120°)

Step 5: Complete the calculation.

R = √(4225 + 2304 - 3120)

R = √3409

R ≈ 58.4 N

Step 6: Select the nearest option.

58.4 N ≈ 59 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (81 N) is incorrect. It may result from using an incorrect trigonometric value or ignoring the effect of the 120° angle.
Option C (113 N) is incorrect because it is simply the arithmetic sum of the two forces (65 N + 48 N). This would only be correct if both forces acted in exactly the same direction.
Option D (97 N) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect application of the formula or treating the forces as though they acted at a much smaller angle.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever two vectors act simultaneously at an angle, first identify the included angle before attempting any calculation.

Remember these important facts:

• Angle 0° gives the maximum possible resultant.
• Angle 180° gives the minimum possible resultant.
• Angle 90° removes the cosine term because cos90° = 0.
• Angles greater than 90° have a negative cosine value, reducing the resultant.

In JAMB, many mistakes occur because candidates either add the forces directly or ignore the cosine of the included angle.
explanation.final_answer: A. 59 N
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two rescue teams pulling the same vehicle from different directions.

Because they are not pulling along the same straight line, their efforts do not add directly. Some of each team's pulling force works against the other because the angle between the ropes is 120°.

The parallelogram law combines both the magnitudes and the directions of the forces to produce one equivalent pulling force. After calculation, the combined pull is approximately 59 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how simultaneous forces are combined graphically and mathematically. The tutorial includes worked examples involving rescue operations, engineering applications, transportation, and other JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
During the installation of a heavy pipeline on an offshore oil and gas platform, two support cables pull simultaneously on the same lifting hook from different directions to keep the pipe stable while it is being raised.

The engineering supervisor instructs the crew to determine the combined effect of the two pulls before lifting begins.

Which principle of vector addition is most appropriate for analysing this situation?

options.A: Parallelogram law of vectors, because the two forces act simultaneously at the same point
options.B: Triangle law of vectors, because the two forces must always act one after the other
options.C: Simple algebraic addition, because forces can be added without considering direction
options.D: Vector subtraction, because simultaneous forces always oppose one another

answer_text: Parallelogram law of vectors, because the two forces act simultaneously at the same point

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to recognise a real-life engineering situation where the parallelogram law of vectors is the appropriate method for combining two concurrent forces acting simultaneously.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors is used to determine the resultant of two vectors that act simultaneously from the same point.

A vector is a physical quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, displacement, velocity and acceleration.

When two forces act on the same object at the same time and from the same point, they are called concurrent forces.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add the numbers?'

Unlike scalar quantities, vectors depend on direction as well as magnitude. Two forces pulling in different directions do not produce the same effect as two forces acting along the same straight line.

The parallelogram law accounts for both the magnitudes of the forces and the angle between them, making it suitable for analysing simultaneous forces in engineering, construction, marine operations, lifting systems and many other practical applications.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the situation described.

The two support cables pull on the same lifting hook at the same time. Their forces are therefore acting simultaneously on a single point.

Step 2: Recall the appropriate vector principle.

Whenever two vectors act simultaneously from a common point, the parallelogram law provides the correct graphical and mathematical method for finding their resultant.

Step 3: Examine Option A.

This correctly identifies the parallelogram law and gives the correct reason why it is applicable. Therefore, Option A is correct.

Step 4: Examine the remaining options.

The triangle law is commonly applied when vectors are added successively using the head-to-tail method, not because simultaneous forces cannot be analysed. Although both methods can produce the same resultant mathematically, the scenario described specifically involves concurrent forces acting together, making the parallelogram law the most appropriate choice.

Simple arithmetic addition ignores direction and is only valid when vectors act along the same straight line in the same direction.

Vector subtraction is used only when determining the difference between vectors or when vectors act in opposite senses under appropriate conditions. Simultaneous forces do not necessarily oppose each other.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it assumes the triangle law is used simply because forces are added. In this engineering situation involving concurrent forces acting simultaneously at one point, the parallelogram law is the standard and most appropriate representation.
Option C is incorrect because vectors cannot generally be added by ordinary arithmetic. Their directions must also be considered.
Option D is incorrect because simultaneous forces are not always opposite in direction. Their combined effect is obtained through vector addition, not automatic subtraction.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question mentions two forces, tensions, pulls, thrusts or velocities acting simultaneously on the same object from a common point, think first of the parallelogram law of vectors.

Do not be misled into adding magnitudes directly. Always ask yourself whether direction and the angle between the vectors must also be considered.
explanation.final_answer: A. Parallelogram law of vectors, because the two forces act simultaneously at the same point.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two tugboats pulling a large floating platform using two ropes attached to the same point.

The platform moves according to the combined effect of both pulls, not just one of them. The parallelogram law helps engineers determine this combined force, called the resultant, by considering both the strength and the direction of each pull.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how concurrent vectors are combined using graphical construction and calculations. The tutorial includes engineering, transportation, marine and lifting-system examples with fully worked JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
An engineering laboratory group investigated the addition of two concurrent force vectors using the parallelogram law. After completing the graphical construction, one student concluded:

"The diagonal drawn from the point where the two vectors start represents the combined effect of both forces because it gives the resultant vector."

Which option best explains whether the student's conclusion is correct?

options.A: The conclusion is correct because the diagonal drawn from the common point of the two vectors represents the resultant vector.
options.B: The conclusion is incorrect because the longest side of the parallelogram always represents the resultant vector.
options.C: The conclusion is incorrect because the parallelogram law can only be applied when the two vectors are perpendicular.
options.D: The conclusion is incorrect because the resultant vector is obtained by adding only the magnitudes of the two vectors.

answer_text: The conclusion is correct because the diagonal drawn from the common point of the two vectors represents the resultant vector.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to analyse a student's interpretation of the parallelogram law of vectors rather than perform a calculation. You are required to determine whether the student's conclusion about the resultant vector is scientifically correct.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law states that when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point, they can be represented by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal of the parallelogram drawn from their common point represents the resultant vector.

A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction.

The resultant vector is the single vector that has the same overall effect as all the individual vectors acting together.

A beginner may ask, "Why is the diagonal used?"

The diagonal naturally combines both the magnitudes and directions of the two vectors. It therefore represents the overall effect of the simultaneous vectors, not merely their numerical values.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what the student described.

The student referred to the diagonal drawn from the common starting point of the two vectors after constructing a parallelogram.

Step 2: Recall the parallelogram law.

According to the parallelogram law, the two vectors are drawn from the same point as adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal originating from that same point represents the resultant vector.

Step 3: Compare the student's statement with the principle.

The student's explanation agrees exactly with the definition of the parallelogram law because the diagonal represents the combined effect of both vectors.

Step 4: Select the best explanation.

Since the student's conclusion is scientifically correct, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the resultant is not determined by whichever side of the parallelogram is longest. It is specifically represented by the diagonal drawn from the common point.
Option C is incorrect because the parallelogram law applies to vectors acting at any included angle, not only at 90°.
Option D is incorrect because vectors cannot generally be combined by adding magnitudes alone. Their directions must also be taken into account.
common_misconceptions: Many students mistakenly believe the longest side of the parallelogram represents the resultant.
Some think the parallelogram law is applicable only to perpendicular vectors.
Others assume vectors are combined by adding magnitudes without considering direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question refers to the parallelogram law, remember these key ideas:

• The vectors must act from a common point.
• They form the adjacent sides of a parallelogram.
• The diagonal drawn from the common point represents the resultant vector.

Do not confuse the diagonal with any side of the parallelogram, and do not ignore the directions of the vectors.
explanation.final_answer: A. The conclusion is correct because the diagonal drawn from the common point of the two vectors represents the resultant vector.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two people pushing a heavy machine at the same time but in different directions.

Instead of considering each push separately, both pushes can be replaced by one imaginary push that produces the same overall effect. In the parallelogram method, this single equivalent push is represented by the diagonal drawn from the point where both pushes begin.

That is why the student's conclusion is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how concurrent vectors are represented graphically, why the diagonal gives the resultant vector, common examination misconceptions, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice.

question: An aircraft is fitted with two auxiliary thrust units that operate simultaneously during take-off. One unit produces a thrust of 280 N while the other produces a thrust of 360 N. If the angle between the two thrust vectors is 30°, what is the magnitude of the resultant thrust acting on the aircraft according to the parallelogram law of vectors?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the resultant of two concurrent vectors acting at an angle using the parallelogram law of vectors. The two thrust forces act simultaneously on the aircraft, so they must be combined using the appropriate vector formula rather than by simple arithmetic addition.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors states that when two vectors act simultaneously from the same point, they can be represented as two adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal drawn from their common point gives the magnitude and direction of the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can't we simply add 280 N and 360 N?'

The answer is that force is a vector quantity. A vector has both magnitude and direction. Since the two thrusts act at an angle of 30° to each other, their directions are different. Therefore, their magnitudes cannot be added directly.

For two vectors of magnitudes P and Q separated by an angle θ, the magnitude of the resultant R is given by:

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

where:

• R = resultant vector
• P = magnitude of the first vector
• Q = magnitude of the second vector
• θ = included angle between the vectors

This formula is obtained from the cosine rule applied to the parallelogram formed by the two vectors.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

First thrust, P = 280 N

Second thrust, Q = 360 N

Included angle, θ = 30°

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

R = √[(280)² + (360)² + 2 × 280 × 360 × cos30°]

Step 5: Find the square root.

R ≈ 618.5 N

To the nearest whole-number option provided,

R ≈ 620 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (640 N) is incorrect because it is simply the arithmetic sum of 280 N and 360 N. Direct addition is only correct when both vectors act in exactly the same direction (angle = 0°).

Option C (460 N) is incorrect because it underestimates the resultant. A small included angle such as 30° produces a resultant much closer to the sum of the two vectors than to either individual vector.

Option D (80 N) is incorrect because it is obtained by subtracting the two magnitudes (360 N − 280 N). Subtraction is appropriate only when vectors act in exactly opposite directions (180°), not when they are separated by 30°.
common_misconceptions: Assuming vector quantities are always added arithmetically.
Ignoring the included angle between vectors.
Using subtraction simply because one force is smaller than the other.
Applying the parallelogram law without using the cosine term.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever two forces, velocities or other vectors act simultaneously from the same point at an angle, think of the parallelogram law.

Remember these special cases:

• θ = 0° → Resultant = P + Q
• θ = 180° → Resultant = |P − Q|
• θ = 90° → Use Pythagoras because cos90° = 0

For every other angle, apply:

R = √(P² + Q² + 2PQ cos θ)

In JAMB examinations, always check whether the question asks for the nearest value after your calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two engines pushing an aircraft at slightly different directions.

Because the pushes are not in exactly the same direction, their effects combine to produce one overall push called the resultant thrust.

The parallelogram law helps us calculate this single equivalent thrust.

Using the given values (280 N, 360 N and 30°), the resultant is approximately 618.5 N, which is closest to 620 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to learn how concurrent vectors are combined, why the cosine rule is used, how to solve worked examples involving forces at different angles, and how to answer JAMB-style calculation questions confidently. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons, practice exercises and exam-focused revision.

question:
A renewable energy engineer made the following statements while analysing the forces acting simultaneously on the support structure of a wind turbine:

Assertion (A): The parallelogram law can be used to determine the resultant of two forces acting simultaneously at a point.

Reason (R): In the parallelogram law, the diagonal drawn from the common point of the two vectors represents their resultant.

Which of the following options is correct?

options.A: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
options.B: Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
options.C: The Assertion is true, but the Reason is false.
options.D: The Assertion is false, but the Reason is true.

answer_text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of the principle behind the parallelogram law of vectors using an Assertion–Reason format. Rather than performing a calculation, you are required to evaluate whether each statement is correct and whether the second statement explains the first.
explanation.principle:
The parallelogram law of vectors is a graphical method used to combine two vectors acting simultaneously at the same point.

A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, displacement and velocity.

When two forces act at the same point but in different directions, they produce a single combined effect called the **resultant vector**.

The parallelogram law states that if the two vectors are represented by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram drawn from the same point, then the diagonal drawn from that common point represents the magnitude and direction of the resultant vector.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is the diagonal used?'

The diagonal naturally combines the effects of both vectors because it extends from their common point to the opposite corner of the parallelogram. It therefore represents the single vector that would have the same overall effect as the two original vectors acting together.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the Assertion.

The Assertion states that the parallelogram law can be used to determine the resultant of two forces acting simultaneously at a point.

This statement is correct. The parallelogram law was developed specifically for combining concurrent vectors such as forces acting simultaneously.

Step 2: Examine the Reason.

The Reason states that the diagonal drawn from the common point of the two vectors represents their resultant.

This is also correct because it is the fundamental construction used in the parallelogram law.

Step 3: Determine whether the Reason explains the Assertion.

The Assertion is true because the resultant is obtained by constructing a parallelogram and drawing its diagonal from the common point.

Therefore, the Reason correctly explains why the parallelogram law is used to obtain the resultant.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Both statements are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the Reason is not merely true; it directly explains why the parallelogram law determines the resultant of two simultaneous vectors.
Option C is incorrect because the Reason is a correct statement describing the graphical construction of the parallelogram law.
Option D is incorrect because the Assertion is also true. The parallelogram law is one of the standard methods for finding the resultant of two concurrent vectors.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Assertion–Reason questions, do not stop after deciding whether each statement is true.

Always ask a second question:

'Does the Reason explain the Assertion?'

For the parallelogram law, remember these key ideas:

• It applies to vectors acting simultaneously at the same point.

• The vectors form two adjacent sides of a parallelogram.

• The diagonal from the common point represents the resultant.

Students often lose marks by recognising that both statements are true but failing to decide whether one explains the other.
explanation.final_answer: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two ropes pulling a wind turbine support from different directions at the same time.

Instead of considering each pull separately, both pulls can be replaced by one imaginary pull that has exactly the same effect. The parallelogram law helps us find this single equivalent pull.

To do this, the two force vectors are drawn from the same point as adjacent sides of a parallelogram. The diagonal starting from that common point gives the resultant force.

This is why both the Assertion and the Reason are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Parallelogram Law of Vectors to study graphical vector addition, concurrent forces, construction of the parallelogram, interpretation of the resultant, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
During the installation of a tower crane at a construction site, an engineer analysed the tension in a supporting cable that was inclined to the ground. Instead of working with the inclined force directly, the engineer first resolved it into horizontal and vertical components.

Why is this approach useful?

options.A: It makes it easier to analyse the effect of the force in mutually perpendicular directions.
options.B: It increases the magnitude of the original force so that calculations become more accurate.
options.C: It changes the inclined force into two independent forces that act separately from the original force.
options.D: It eliminates the need to consider the direction of the force during calculations.

answer_text: It makes it easier to analyse the effect of the force in mutually perpendicular directions.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the purpose of resolving a vector into horizontal and vertical components before solving practical engineering problems.
explanation.principle:
Many physical quantities, such as force, velocity, displacement and acceleration, are vectors because they possess both magnitude and direction.

When a vector acts at an angle, analysing its effect directly can be difficult. To simplify the analysis, the vector is resolved into two components that act at right angles (perpendicular) to each other, usually a horizontal component and a vertical component.

A beginner may ask, 'What is vector resolution?'

Vector resolution is the process of expressing a single vector as two or more perpendicular component vectors whose combined effect is exactly the same as that of the original vector.

Another beginner may ask, 'Does resolving a vector create new forces?'

No. The horizontal and vertical components are not additional forces. They are simply different representations of the same original vector. When these perpendicular components are combined, they reproduce the original vector exactly.

Resolving vectors allows engineers and physicists to analyse motion and forces independently along each axis. This greatly simplifies calculations involving equilibrium, motion, structures and machines.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the engineering task.

The engineer is dealing with a force that acts at an angle. Such a force has effects in both the horizontal and vertical directions.

Step 2: Recall the purpose of vector resolution.

The purpose is not to change the force but to express it as perpendicular components that are easier to analyse separately.

Step 3: Examine Option A.

This option correctly states that resolving the vector simplifies analysis by separating its effects into mutually perpendicular directions. This is the fundamental reason vector resolution is used.

Step 4: Examine the remaining options.

None of them correctly describes what vector resolution does. They either suggest that the force changes, increases or loses its direction, which is incorrect.

Step 5: Choose the correct answer.

The correct option is A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because resolving a vector does not increase or decrease its magnitude. The original vector remains unchanged; it is only expressed in terms of components.
Option C is incorrect because the horizontal and vertical components are not separate new forces. Together, they represent the same original inclined force.
Option D is incorrect because direction is one of the most important characteristics of a vector. Resolving a vector helps account for direction rather than eliminating it.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks why vectors are resolved, remember that the objective is to simplify the analysis of vectors acting at an angle.

Keep these facts in mind:

• Resolution does not change the original vector.
• The components are perpendicular to each other.
• The components combine to produce the original vector.
• Vector resolution is useful for forces, velocities, accelerations, displacements and other vector quantities.

Avoid the common misconception that vector resolution creates additional forces or changes the magnitude of the original vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. It makes it easier to analyse the effect of the force in mutually perpendicular directions.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a heavy object with a rope that slopes upward.

Part of your pull moves the object forward, while another part pulls upward. Instead of studying the sloping pull all at once, it is easier to separate it into a horizontal part and a vertical part.

This process is called vector resolution. It makes calculations easier without changing the original force.

That is why Option A is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into components, how horizontal and vertical components are obtained, the role of perpendicular axes, and how vector resolution is applied in engineering, construction, transportation and other JAMB-style problem-solving situations. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and worked examples.

question:
During the installation of a roof truss, a support cable exerts a force of 50 N at an angle of 37° above the horizontal. What is the horizontal component of the force?

(Take cos 37° = 0.80.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to resolve an inclined force into its horizontal and vertical components. Specifically, you are required to determine the horizontal component of a 50 N force acting at an angle of 37° above the horizontal.
explanation.principle:
Many forces in nature do not act purely horizontally or vertically. Instead, they act at an angle. To analyse such forces more easily, physicists split, or resolve, the force into two mutually perpendicular components:

• Horizontal component
• Vertical component

These two components together produce exactly the same effect as the original force.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal component is obtained using the cosine of the angle, while the vertical component is obtained using the sine of the angle.
explanation.definitions:
Vector resolution is the process of breaking a single vector into two perpendicular components that together are equivalent to the original vector.

The horizontal component represents the part of the vector acting parallel to the horizontal direction, while the vertical component represents the part acting vertically upward or downward.
explanation.formula_introduction:
If a vector of magnitude F makes an angle θ with the horizontal, then:

Horizontal component = F cos θ

Vertical component = F sin θ

where:

F = magnitude of the vector
θ = angle measured from the horizontal.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Force, F = 50 N

Angle, θ = 37°

cos 37° = 0.80

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Horizontal component of the force.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Horizontal component = F cos θ

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Horizontal component = 50 × 0.80

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Horizontal component = 40 N

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

40 N corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (30 N) is incorrect. It could result from using an incorrect trigonometric ratio or an incorrect calculation.
Option C (50 N) is incorrect because it is the magnitude of the original force. A component of a vector acting at an angle must be smaller than the original vector.
Option D (62.5 N) is incorrect because a component cannot be greater than the magnitude of the original force. This value could arise from dividing by the cosine instead of multiplying by it.
common_misconceptions: Using sine instead of cosine when the angle is measured from the horizontal.
Thinking the horizontal component is equal to the original force.
Dividing by the trigonometric ratio instead of multiplying.
Believing that one component can be greater than the original vector.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always identify where the angle is measured from before choosing a trigonometric ratio.

• Angle from the horizontal → horizontal component = F cos θ.
• Angle from the horizontal → vertical component = F sin θ.

In JAMB examinations, many candidates lose marks simply by interchanging sine and cosine.
explanation.final_answer: B. 40 N
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a roof support cable that slopes upward. Not all of your pulling force moves the structure horizontally because part of the force acts upward. By multiplying the force by the cosine of the angle, you find the portion acting horizontally.

Therefore, the horizontal component is 40 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how inclined forces are resolved into horizontal and vertical components using sine and cosine, with beginner-friendly explanations, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
At an airport baggage handling area, a conveyor belt pulls a suitcase along an inclined path. A technician explains that the pulling force can be resolved into a horizontal component and a vertical component.

What does this description imply about the two components of the pulling force?

options.A: They are two perpendicular parts whose combined effect is equal to the original pulling force.
options.B: They are two independent forces acting separately on the suitcase.
options.C: Each component has the same magnitude as the original pulling force but acts in a different direction.
options.D: The horizontal component alone produces exactly the same effect as the original pulling force.

answer_text: They are two perpendicular parts whose combined effect is equal to the original pulling force.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of what it means to resolve a vector into horizontal and vertical components. Rather than performing calculations, you are required to interpret the physical meaning of vector components in a practical engineering situation.
explanation.principle:
Many physical quantities such as force, velocity and displacement are vectors because they possess both magnitude and direction.

Sometimes a vector acts at an angle, making it difficult to analyse its effect directly. To simplify the analysis, the vector is resolved into two mutually perpendicular components—usually a horizontal component and a vertical component.

A beginner may ask, 'Does resolving a vector create new forces?'

No. Vector resolution does not create additional forces. Instead, it expresses one original vector as two perpendicular parts that, when combined, reproduce exactly the same vector.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why must the components be perpendicular?'

Perpendicular components are independent of one another, making it easier to analyse motion or forces separately along the horizontal and vertical directions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what is being described.

The technician says that the pulling force is resolved into horizontal and vertical components. This means the original force has been split into two perpendicular parts for easier analysis.

Step 2: Recall the purpose of vector resolution.

The horizontal and vertical components together represent exactly the same force as the original inclined force. They are not additional forces but parts of the same vector.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option A correctly states that the components are perpendicular parts whose combined effect is equal to the original pulling force.

Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the components are not separate independent forces. They are simply different directional parts of one original vector.
Option C is incorrect because each component is smaller than or, at most, equal to the original vector. A component cannot generally have the same magnitude as the original vector.
Option D is incorrect because the horizontal component alone cannot replace the original inclined force. Both the horizontal and vertical components are required to reproduce the original vector.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB refers to the horizontal and vertical components of a vector, remember that they are perpendicular parts of a single vector. Do not mistake them for separate forces acting independently. The original vector is obtained only when both components are combined.
explanation.final_answer: A. They are two perpendicular parts whose combined effect is equal to the original pulling force.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a trolley up a ramp. Instead of looking at the push as one slanted force, you can think of it as having two parts:

• one acting horizontally;
• one acting vertically.

These two parts are at right angles to each other. When they are combined, they produce exactly the same slanted push you started with. They are not new forces—they are simply different parts of the same original force.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, how perpendicular components are interpreted, and how vector resolution is applied in engineering, transportation, navigation and JAMB-style examination questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and worked examples.

question:
During the design of a pedestrian bridge, two structural engineers discussed how to determine the combined effect of two forces acting at an angle on a support beam.

Engineer I suggested resolving each force into horizontal and vertical components before combining the components.

Engineer II suggested using the parallelogram law directly to obtain the resultant of the two forces.

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: Only Engineer I's method is correct because the parallelogram law cannot be used for forces acting at an angle.
options.B: Only Engineer II's method is correct because vectors should never be resolved into components.
options.C: Both methods are correct because they can produce the same resultant when applied correctly.
options.D: Neither method is correct because forces acting at an angle must always be added arithmetically.

answer_text: Both methods are correct because they can produce the same resultant when applied correctly.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares two different methods used to solve force problems involving vectors. It asks whether resolving vectors into perpendicular components and using the parallelogram law are alternative approaches that lead to the same resultant.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. When two or more vectors act together, they may be combined to obtain a single equivalent vector known as the resultant.

There are several accepted methods of finding the resultant of vectors. Two of the most common are:

• The parallelogram law.
