• Resolution of vectors into horizontal and vertical components.

A beginner may ask, 'Why are there different methods?'

Different methods are more convenient in different situations. The parallelogram law provides a graphical or geometrical approach, while vector resolution converts each vector into perpendicular components that can easily be added algebraically.

Although the procedures differ, both methods are based on the same principles of vector addition and will produce the same resultant when applied correctly.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Engineer I's method.

Resolving each force into horizontal and vertical components is a standard analytical technique used in physics and engineering.

After resolving the forces, the horizontal components are added together and the vertical components are added together to determine the resultant.

Therefore, Engineer I's method is correct.

Step 2: Examine Engineer II's method.

The parallelogram law is another recognised method for adding two vectors acting at an angle.

By constructing a parallelogram using the two vectors as adjacent sides, the diagonal represents the resultant vector.

Therefore, Engineer II's method is also correct.

Step 3: Compare the two methods.

The methods differ only in approach.

• Vector resolution is usually preferred for calculations involving components.
• The parallelogram law is useful for graphical representation and for understanding how vectors combine.

When performed correctly, both methods give the same resultant vector.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the parallelogram law is a recognised method for combining two vectors acting at an angle.
Option B is incorrect because resolving vectors into perpendicular components is one of the most widely used analytical methods in mechanics.
Option D is incorrect because vectors cannot generally be added by simply adding their magnitudes. Direction must also be taken into account.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, questions may ask you to compare different vector methods rather than perform calculations.

Remember these key facts:

• Vector resolution and the parallelogram law are complementary methods.
• Both produce the same resultant when used correctly.
• Resolution is generally more convenient for numerical calculations involving forces, velocities and displacements.
• The parallelogram law is especially useful for visualising how two vectors combine.

Do not assume that every force problem must first be solved by resolving vectors into components. Choose the method that best suits the problem.
explanation.final_answer: C. Both methods are correct because they can produce the same resultant when applied correctly.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two friends pulling a heavy cart using ropes tied at different angles.

One engineer decides to split each pulling force into horizontal and vertical parts before adding them.

Another engineer draws a parallelogram to combine the two pulls.

Although the methods look different, they both determine the same overall pulling force. They are simply two valid ways of solving the same vector problem.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors for a complete lesson on resolving vectors into components, comparing analytical and graphical methods, understanding when to use the parallelogram law, and solving JAMB-style force problems with detailed worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly explanations, practice questions, and examination tips.

question:
In a warehouse, a worker pulls a loaded trolley using a rope with a force of 72 N. The rope makes an angle of 40° with the horizontal floor.

What is the horizontal component of the pulling force?

(Take cos 40° = 0.77.)

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to resolve an inclined force into its horizontal component. Although the worker pulls with a force of 72 N, only the part of the force acting horizontally moves the trolley forward.
explanation.principle:
Many forces in Physics act at an angle rather than purely horizontally or vertically. To determine how much of such a force acts in a particular direction, the force is resolved into two perpendicular components:

• Horizontal component
• Vertical component

Resolving a vector means splitting one vector into two mutually perpendicular vectors whose combined effect is exactly the same as the original vector.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

Horizontal component = F cos θ

Vertical component = F sin θ

where:

• F = magnitude of the original force
• θ = angle the force makes with the horizontal

The cosine function is used for the horizontal component because it represents the side adjacent to the given angle in a right-angled triangle.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known quantities.

Magnitude of force, F = 72 N

Angle with the horizontal, θ = 40°

cos 40° = 0.77

Step 2: Identify the quantity required.

We need the horizontal component of the force.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Horizontal component = F cos θ

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Horizontal component = 72 × 0.77

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

72 × 0.77 = 55.44 N

≈ 55.4 N

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

55.4 N corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (46.3 N) is incorrect. It results from using an incorrect trigonometric ratio or an arithmetic error during multiplication.
Option C (61.7 N) is incorrect. It is a plausible distractor obtained from using an incorrect value for cos 40° or rounding improperly.
Option D (72.0 N) is incorrect because it is the magnitude of the original pulling force. The horizontal component must be smaller than the original force since the force acts at an angle.
common_misconceptions: Using sine instead of cosine when the angle is measured from the horizontal.
Assuming the horizontal component is equal to the original force.
Forgetting that a component of a vector is always less than the magnitude of the original vector unless the angle is 0°.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a force makes an angle with the horizontal, immediately identify which component is required.

• Horizontal component → use cosine.
• Vertical component → use sine.

A quick memory aid is:

'H for Horizontal goes with Cosine when the angle is from the Horizontal.'
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a trolley with a rope that is slanted upward. Not all of your pulling force moves the trolley forward. Part of the force lifts upward, while the remaining part pushes the trolley horizontally.

The forward (horizontal) part is obtained using:

Horizontal component = Force × cos(angle)

= 72 × 0.77

= 55.4 N

Therefore, the horizontal component of the pulling force is 55.4 N.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how inclined forces are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, when to use sine or cosine, common examination mistakes, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and additional practice.

question:
During a physics laboratory practical, a learner was asked to resolve a 64 N force acting at an angle of 30° above the horizontal into its horizontal and vertical components.

The learner wrote:

Horizontal component = 64 sin30°

Vertical component = 64 cos30°

Assuming the angle is correctly measured from the horizontal, what mistake did the learner make?

options.A: The sine and cosine ratios were interchanged for the given reference angle.
options.B: The force should first have been converted to kilograms before resolving it.
options.C: The horizontal and vertical components should be added before applying trigonometric ratios.
options.D: Vector resolution can only be carried out when the force acts at 45°.

answer_text: The sine and cosine ratios were interchanged for the given reference angle.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify a common error made when resolving a vector into perpendicular components. Rather than carrying out a calculation, you are required to analyse a learner's method and determine why it is incorrect.
explanation.principle:
Vector resolution is the process of splitting a single vector into two mutually perpendicular components, usually horizontal and vertical. These components combine to produce the original vector.

Before using any trigonometric ratio, you must identify the reference angle. The reference angle is the angle between the vector and one of the coordinate axes.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = F cosθ
• Vertical component = F sinθ

where:

F = magnitude of the vector
θ = angle measured from the horizontal

The cosine ratio gives the component adjacent to the reference angle, while the sine ratio gives the component opposite the reference angle.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the reference axis.

The question states that the 30° angle is measured from the horizontal direction.

Step 2: Recall the correct expressions.

For an angle measured from the horizontal:

Horizontal component = F cosθ

Vertical component = F sinθ

Step 3: Compare the learner's method.

The learner wrote:

Horizontal component = 64 sin30°

Vertical component = 64 cos30°

These expressions are reversed.

Step 4: Identify the misconception.

The learner confused the trigonometric ratios by assigning sine to the adjacent side and cosine to the opposite side. This is one of the most common mistakes made when resolving vectors.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

The error is that the sine and cosine ratios were interchanged.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because force is already measured in newtons (N). There is no need to convert it to kilograms before resolving it into components.
Option C is incorrect because vector components are obtained first. They are not added before applying trigonometric ratios.
Option D is incorrect because vector resolution can be carried out for any angle. It is not restricted to 45°.
explanation.common_misconception: Many students memorise formulas without first identifying the reference angle. As a result, they interchange sine and cosine or use the correct formulas with the wrong angle. Always determine whether the given angle is measured from the horizontal or the vertical before selecting the appropriate trigonometric ratio.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, carefully note where the angle is measured from.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal → cosine
• Vertical → sine

If the angle is measured from the vertical, these relationships are reversed. Drawing a simple right-angled triangle often helps you choose the correct ratio.
explanation.final_answer: A. The sine and cosine ratios were interchanged for the given reference angle.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine shining a torch so that its beam makes an angle with the floor.

The beam can be thought of as having a horizontal part and a vertical part.

When the angle is measured from the floor (horizontal), the horizontal part is always found using cosine, while the vertical part is found using sine.

The learner swapped these two ratios, which is why the solution is incorrect.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how to identify the reference angle, choose the correct trigonometric ratio, resolve vectors into horizontal and vertical components, and avoid common examination mistakes. Continue practising with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed worked examples and JAMB-focused revision.

question:
A farmer uses a tractor to pull a cultivator across a level field. The pulling force has a magnitude of 90 N and acts at an angle of 30° above the horizontal. Assuming the cultivator moves horizontally, which component of the pulling force is responsible for moving it forward, and what is its magnitude?

(Take cos 30° = 0.866 and sin 30° = 0.500.)

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how vector resolution is applied in agriculture. It asks you to identify the component of an inclined pulling force that actually moves farm equipment along a horizontal surface and to calculate its value.
explanation.principle:
Many forces in physics act at an angle rather than purely horizontally or vertically. To understand their effects, the force is resolved into two mutually perpendicular components:

• A horizontal component, which acts parallel to the ground.
• A vertical component, which acts perpendicular to the ground.

When an object moves along a horizontal surface, the horizontal component is the one that produces the forward motion. The vertical component mainly reduces or increases the normal reaction between the object and the ground, but it does not directly move the object forward.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Magnitude of force, F = 90 N

Angle with the horizontal, θ = 30°

cos 30° = 0.866

Step 2: Determine the component required.

Since the cultivator moves horizontally, the effective force responsible for its forward motion is the horizontal component of the pulling force.

Step 3: Introduce the appropriate formula.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal,

Horizontal component = F cos θ

where:
• F is the magnitude of the force.
• θ is the angle the force makes with the horizontal.

Step 4: Substitute the values.

Horizontal component = 90 × 0.866

= 77.94 N

Step 5: Express the answer appropriately.

The effective forward force is approximately 77.9 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because 45.0 N is the vertical component (90 × sin 30°). The vertical component acts upward and does not produce the forward motion.
Option C is incorrect because it incorrectly uses the vertical component as the horizontal component. This mistake usually occurs when sine and cosine are interchanged.
Option D is incorrect because although 77.9 N is a correct numerical value, it is the horizontal component, not the vertical component.
explanation.common_misconception:
A common mistake is to assume that the entire 90 N pulling force moves the cultivator forward. In reality, only the component parallel to the direction of motion contributes directly to the forward movement.

Another frequent error is confusing sine and cosine. Whenever the angle is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal component is obtained using cosine, while the vertical component is obtained using sine.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, always identify the reference direction before choosing the trigonometric ratio.

• Angle measured from the horizontal:
  Horizontal component = F cos θ
  Vertical component = F sin θ

• Angle measured from the vertical:
  Horizontal component = F sin θ
  Vertical component = F cos θ

Before performing any calculation, ask yourself, 'Which component is required?' This simple habit helps avoid using the wrong trigonometric function.
explanation.final_answer: A. Horizontal component, 77.9 N
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a heavy box with a rope that slopes upward.

Part of your pull moves the box forward, while another part slightly lifts it. The forward-moving part is the horizontal component of the force.

Since the tractor is pulling the cultivator across the field, the horizontal component causes the movement.

Using

Horizontal component = 90 × cos 30° = 90 × 0.866 = 77.9 N.

Hence, Option A is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into perpendicular components, how to choose between sine and cosine, and how vector resolution is applied in agriculture, engineering, transportation, construction, and other real-life JAMB-style situations.

question:
An elevator maintenance technician tightens a support cable that exerts a tension of 84 N at an angle of 55° above the horizontal. What is the vertical component of the tension force?

(Take sin 55° = 0.82.)

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to resolve an inclined tension force into its vertical component. You are required to identify the correct trigonometric ratio and calculate the upward part of the force.
explanation.principle:
Many forces in Physics do not act purely horizontally or vertically. Instead, they act at an angle. To understand how such forces influence motion, they are resolved into two perpendicular components:

• Horizontal component
• Vertical component

These two components together are equivalent to the original force.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = F cos θ
• Vertical component = F sin θ

where:

F = magnitude of the force
θ = angle measured from the horizontal.

The vertical component represents the part of the force acting upward or downward. In this problem, the cable is inclined above the horizontal, so the vertical component acts upward.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Magnitude of tension, F = 84 N

Angle with the horizontal, θ = 55°

sin 55° = 0.82

Step 2: Determine the required quantity.

The question asks for the vertical component of the tension force.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Since the angle is measured from the horizontal:

Vertical component = F sin θ

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Vertical component = 84 × 0.82

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

84 × 0.82 = 68.88 N

≈ 68.9 N

Step 6: Compare with the options.

The calculated value corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (48.2 N) is incorrect. It results from an incorrect application of the trigonometric ratio or an arithmetic error.
Option C (84.0 N) is incorrect because the vertical component is only part of the original tension. A component cannot equal the original vector unless the force acts entirely in that direction.
Option D (58.2 N) is incorrect because it does not correspond to the correct evaluation of 84 sin 55° and reflects a common computational mistake.
common_misconceptions: Using cosine instead of sine when the angle is measured from the horizontal.
Assuming the vertical component is always larger than the horizontal component.
Thinking that one component is equal to the entire force.
Ignoring the reference from which the angle is measured.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always determine where the angle is measured before selecting the trigonometric ratio.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component → F cos θ
• Vertical component → F sin θ

If the angle is measured from the vertical, the relationships are reversed. JAMB frequently tests this distinction.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a rope that is slanted upward. Part of your pull moves sideways, while another part lifts upward.

The upward part is called the vertical component. Since the angle is measured from the horizontal, we multiply the force by the sine of the angle.

84 × 0.82 = 68.9 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how inclined forces are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, how to choose between sine and cosine correctly, and how to solve JAMB-style vector resolution problems using step-by-step worked examples at the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
During a physics classroom discussion on resolving vectors into perpendicular components, the teacher asked students to comment on the following statements:

I. Resolving a vector produces two mutually perpendicular components whose combined effect is equivalent to the original vector.

II. The horizontal and vertical components of a resolved vector are independent vectors that can each replace the original vector.

III. Vector resolution can be applied to quantities such as force, velocity and displacement.

IV. The magnitude of either resolved component can never exceed the magnitude of the original vector.

Which of the following options correctly identifies the true statements?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the principles of vector resolution by asking you to determine which statements correctly describe the relationship between a vector and its resolved components.
explanation.principle:
Vector resolution is the process of splitting a single vector into two perpendicular components, usually horizontal and vertical. These components work together to reproduce exactly the same effect as the original vector.

A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, velocity, displacement, acceleration and momentum.

A beginner may ask, 'Why do we resolve a vector?'

Many physical situations become easier to analyse when an inclined vector is replaced by horizontal and vertical components. For example, engineers analyse forces on bridges, pilots study aircraft motion, and physicists investigate projectile motion by considering separate horizontal and vertical effects.

It is important to remember that the resolved components do not replace the original vector individually. Instead, they act together to represent it.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

This statement is correct.

A resolved vector consists of two perpendicular components whose combined effect is exactly equal to that of the original vector.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

This statement is incorrect.

Neither the horizontal component nor the vertical component alone can replace the original vector. Each component represents only part of the original vector, and both are required together.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

This statement is correct.

Vector resolution is not limited to forces. It can also be applied to other vector quantities such as velocity and displacement whenever they act at an angle.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

This statement is correct.

Each resolved component is obtained by multiplying the original vector by the sine or cosine of an angle. Since the values of sine and cosine never exceed 1, the magnitude of an individual component cannot be greater than that of the original vector.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Statements I, III and IV are correct, while Statement II is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement II, which falsely suggests that one component can replace the original vector.
Option C is incorrect because Statement II is incorrect, even though Statements III and IV are correct.
Option D is incorrect because it also includes the incorrect Statement II.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about vector resolution, remember these key facts:

• A resolved vector is represented by two perpendicular components.
• The components act together—not separately—to reproduce the original vector.
• Vector resolution applies to any vector quantity, not just force.
• The magnitude of an individual component is always less than or equal to the magnitude of the original vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a sled using a rope held at an angle.

Part of your pull moves the sled forward, while another part acts upward on the rope. These two parts are called components.

Neither part alone is equal to your original pull, but together they produce exactly the same overall effect. This is why Statements I, III and IV are correct, while Statement II is not.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved, how horizontal and vertical components are obtained, when to use sine or cosine, common mistakes made in examinations, and worked JAMB-style examples with detailed explanations.

question:
During the installation of a telecommunications mast, an engineer represents the tension in a supporting cable by a vector of magnitude F acting at an angle θ above the horizontal.

Which of the following expressions correctly gives the horizontal component of the tension?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the correct mathematical expression used to resolve a vector into its horizontal component when the angle is measured from the horizontal.
explanation.principle:
Vector resolution is the process of breaking a single vector into two mutually perpendicular components, usually one horizontal and one vertical.

A beginner may ask, 'Why do we split a vector into components?'

Many physical situations become easier to analyse when forces or velocities acting at an angle are separated into horizontal and vertical parts. These components together produce exactly the same effect as the original vector.

Suppose a vector of magnitude F makes an angle θ with the horizontal.

The two components are:

• Horizontal component = F cos θ
• Vertical component = F sin θ

This follows directly from the definitions of cosine and sine in a right-angled triangle.

Cosine relates the adjacent side to the hypotenuse, while sine relates the opposite side to the hypotenuse. Since the horizontal side is adjacent to the given angle, cosine is used.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the reference line.

The angle θ is measured from the horizontal.

Step 2: Determine which side represents the horizontal component.

The horizontal component lies adjacent to the given angle.

Step 3: Recall the trigonometric relationship.

For a right-angled triangle:

cos θ = Adjacent/Hypotenuse

Step 4: Rearrange the expression.

Adjacent = Hypotenuse × cos θ

Therefore,

Horizontal component = F cos θ.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option A matches the correct expression.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because F sin θ gives the vertical component when the angle is measured from the horizontal.
Option C is incorrect because tangent relates the opposite side to the adjacent side and is not used directly to obtain a vector component from its magnitude.
Option D is incorrect because secant is the reciprocal of cosine and is not used for resolving vectors into components.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always identify the reference angle before choosing between sine and cosine.

• If the angle is measured from the horizontal:
  Horizontal component = F cos θ
  Vertical component = F sin θ

• If the angle is measured from the vertical:
  Horizontal component = F sin θ
  Vertical component = F cos θ

Many JAMB candidates lose marks because they ignore the reference line and interchange sine with cosine.
explanation.final_answer: A. F cos θ
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a cable that is slanted upward.

Part of the pull acts horizontally, while another part acts vertically.

Since the angle is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal part is next to the angle. In trigonometry, the adjacent side is obtained using cosine.

Therefore, the horizontal component is F cos θ.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into perpendicular components, how to identify the correct reference angle, when to use sine or cosine, and how to solve JAMB-style questions involving inclined forces, velocities and other vector quantities.

question:
An engineer on an offshore oil platform is analysing the tension in a support cable. The cable exerts a force of 96 N at an angle of 25° above the horizontal.

Using sin 25° = 0.423 and cos 25° = 0.906, what are the horizontal and vertical components of the force respectively?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to resolve an inclined force into its horizontal and vertical components. Instead of using the entire force in one direction, you must determine how much of the 96 N acts horizontally and how much acts vertically.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Examples include force, velocity, displacement and acceleration.

Many forces encountered in engineering are inclined rather than perfectly horizontal or vertical. To analyse their effects, the force is resolved into two perpendicular components.

The horizontal component represents the part of the force acting parallel to the horizontal surface, while the vertical component represents the part acting upward or downward.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = F cos θ
• Vertical component = F sin θ

where:
F = magnitude of the vector
θ = angle made with the horizontal

The cosine ratio is used for the horizontal component because it is adjacent to the given angle, while the sine ratio is used for the vertical component because it is opposite the given angle.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Force, F = 96 N
Angle, θ = 25°
cos 25° = 0.906
sin 25° = 0.423

Step 2: Calculate the horizontal component.

Horizontal component = F cos θ
= 96 × 0.906
= 86.976 N
≈ 86.98 N

Step 3: Calculate the vertical component.

Vertical component = F sin θ
= 96 × 0.423
= 40.608 N
≈ 40.61 N

Step 4: Compare the calculated values with the options.

The horizontal component is 86.98 N and the vertical component is 40.61 N.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.

Step 5: Check whether the result is reasonable.

Since the angle is only 25°, the force acts mainly in the horizontal direction. Therefore, the horizontal component should be much larger than the vertical component.

This agrees with the calculated values, confirming that the solution is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it interchanges the horizontal and vertical components. This mistake results from using sine and cosine in the wrong places.
Option C is incorrect because it assumes the entire 96 N acts horizontally. A component can never exceed the original vector or be equal to it unless the force acts exactly along that direction.
Option D is incorrect because it incorrectly takes the original force as the vertical component. The vertical component must be obtained using F sin θ.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always begin by checking where the angle is measured from.

• If the angle is measured from the horizontal:
  Horizontal = F cos θ
  Vertical = F sin θ

• If the angle is measured from the vertical:
  Horizontal = F sin θ
  Vertical = F cos θ

Many JAMB candidates lose marks simply because they use the wrong trigonometric ratio. Before substituting values, identify which side of the right-angled triangle is adjacent and which is opposite to the given angle.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling an object with a rope that slopes upward.

Not all of your pulling force moves the object forward. Part of it pulls the object forward, while another part lifts it slightly.

The forward pull is the horizontal component, and the lifting pull is the vertical component.

For a force of 96 N inclined at 25° above the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = 86.98 N
• Vertical component = 40.61 N

These two perpendicular components together produce the original 96 N force.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how inclined vectors are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, understand when to use sine or cosine, and practise additional JAMB-style worked examples from engineering, transportation, marine operations and industrial applications.

question:
A technology company is testing autonomous drones to deliver medical supplies to remote communities. During one test flight, the drone encounters a steady crosswind blowing from the side. To keep the drone moving accurately along its planned route, the onboard navigation system resolves the drone's velocity into horizontal and vertical components.

What is the primary reason for resolving the drone's velocity into components in this situation?

options.A: To determine how the drone's motion is distributed along perpendicular directions so that the effect of the crosswind can be properly accounted for
options.B: To increase the magnitude of the drone's velocity before it reaches its destination
options.C: To convert the drone's velocity into a scalar quantity for easier calculation
options.D: To eliminate the influence of gravity on the drone's motion

answer_text: To determine how the drone's motion is distributed along perpendicular directions so that the effect of the crosswind can be properly accounted for

explanation.question_restate: This question examines your understanding of why vectors are resolved into perpendicular components in a practical situation involving drone navigation. It focuses on the purpose of vector resolution rather than performing numerical calculations.
explanation.principle:
Many physical quantities, such as force, velocity and displacement, are vectors because they have both magnitude and direction.

In many real-life situations, a vector does not act entirely in one direction. Instead, it acts at an angle. To analyse its effect more easily, the vector is resolved into two mutually perpendicular components, usually a horizontal component and a vertical component.

A beginner may ask, 'Why split one vector into two parts?'

The original vector is not being changed. Instead, it is represented by two perpendicular components whose combined effect is exactly the same as the original vector.

This allows physicists and engineers to study motion or forces separately along each direction. For example, a drone moving forward while being pushed sideways by the wind experiences motion in two independent directions that can be analysed separately.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the physical quantity involved.

The quantity being resolved is velocity, which is a vector because it has both magnitude and direction.

Step 2: Recognise the purpose of vector resolution.

Resolving a vector into perpendicular components makes it easier to analyse motion or forces acting in different directions independently.

Step 3: Apply this idea to the drone.

The drone is intended to move along its planned route, but the crosswind pushes it sideways. By resolving the velocity into horizontal and vertical components, the navigation system can determine how much of the motion contributes to forward movement and how much is affected by the wind.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Option A correctly states the purpose of vector resolution.

Options B, C and D describe effects that vector resolution does not produce.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because resolving a vector does not increase its magnitude. It merely expresses the same vector in terms of perpendicular components.
Option C is incorrect because velocity remains a vector after resolution. Its components are also vectors, not scalars.
Option D is incorrect because resolving a velocity vector does not remove or cancel the effect of gravity. Gravity is a separate force that continues to act on the drone.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a real-life application involving aircraft, boats, drones, cranes, vehicles or projectiles, ask yourself whether motion or force occurs in more than one direction.

If it does, vector resolution is often the appropriate method because it allows each perpendicular direction to be analysed independently.
explanation.final_answer: A. To determine how the drone's motion is distributed along perpendicular directions so that the effect of the crosswind can be properly accounted for
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a toy car forward while someone else gently pushes it sideways.

The car does not move in only one direction. To understand its motion, we separate it into a forward part and a sideways part.

That is exactly what vector resolution does. It breaks one vector into two perpendicular components without changing the original motion. This helps engineers and navigation systems predict and control movement accurately.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into components, how horizontal and vertical components are interpreted, and how vector resolution is applied in engineering, aviation, marine navigation, robotics and JAMB-style examination questions.

question:
During an engineering workshop, an apprentice was asked to resolve an inclined force into horizontal and vertical components. He argued that since cosine is usually greater than sine for small angles, the horizontal component should always be obtained using cosine, regardless of the angle from which the measurement is taken.

Which explanation best evaluates the apprentice's reasoning?

options.A: The apprentice is correct because the horizontal component is always greater than the vertical component.
options.B: The apprentice is correct because cosine should always be used to determine the horizontal component of a vector.
options.C: The apprentice is incorrect because the appropriate trigonometric ratio depends on the reference angle from which the vector is measured.
options.D: The apprentice is incorrect because vectors can only be resolved using the parallelogram law.

answer_text: The apprentice is incorrect because the appropriate trigonometric ratio depends on the reference angle from which the vector is measured.

explanation.question_restate: This question examines whether you understand how to choose the correct trigonometric ratio when resolving a vector into perpendicular components. Rather than performing a calculation, it tests your ability to analyse a common mistake made by students.
explanation.principle:
Resolving a vector means expressing a single vector as two perpendicular components, usually horizontal and vertical.

These components together produce exactly the same effect as the original vector.

When resolving a vector, the choice between sine and cosine is not based on which function has the larger numerical value. Instead, it depends entirely on the reference angle.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal component is the adjacent side and is found using cosine, while the vertical component is the opposite side and is found using sine.

If the angle is measured from the vertical, the situation is reversed. The vertical component becomes the adjacent side and is obtained using cosine, while the horizontal component becomes the opposite side and is obtained using sine.

Therefore, the reference angle determines the correct trigonometric ratio.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the apprentice's claim.

He assumes that cosine should always be used for the horizontal component simply because it often gives a larger value for small angles.

Step 2: Recall the rule for resolving vectors.

The correct trigonometric ratio depends on which side of the right triangle is adjacent or opposite to the given angle.

Step 3: Consider different reference angles.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal, cosine gives the horizontal component.

If the angle is measured from the vertical, sine gives the horizontal component instead.

Step 4: Evaluate the reasoning.

The apprentice ignored the importance of the reference angle. His method would produce incorrect components whenever the angle is measured from a different direction.

Step 5: Select the best explanation.

Option C correctly explains why the apprentice's reasoning is flawed.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the horizontal component is not always larger than the vertical component. Their relative sizes depend on the angle of inclination.
Option B is incorrect because cosine is not universally used for the horizontal component. The correct ratio depends on the chosen reference angle.
Option D is incorrect because the parallelogram law is a method for combining vectors, not the only method for resolving a vector into components.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you resolve a vector, first identify the angle that has been given. Then determine which side of the right triangle is adjacent to that angle and which is opposite.

Never decide between sine and cosine based on memory alone. Always relate the trigonometric ratio to the reference angle shown or described in the question.
explanation.final_answer: C. The apprentice is incorrect because the appropriate trigonometric ratio depends on the reference angle from which the vector is measured.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a ladder leaning against a wall.

If someone tells you the angle between the ladder and the ground, the horizontal and vertical distances are found using one arrangement of sine and cosine.

If the angle is instead measured between the ladder and the wall, the roles of the two ratios change.

This is why you must always pay attention to where the angle is measured from. The apprentice forgot this important rule.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors for a detailed explanation of vector components, reference angles, choosing the correct trigonometric ratio, common examination mistakes, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons and additional practice questions.

question: A ferry travels across a lake with a speed of 22 m s⁻¹ in a direction that makes an angle of 30° with the eastward direction. What is the eastward component of the ferry's velocity?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the component of a boat's velocity along the eastward direction by resolving the given velocity vector into its horizontal and vertical components.
explanation.principle:
Velocity is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

When a vector acts at an angle, it can be resolved into two mutually perpendicular components. These components describe how much of the vector acts along each chosen direction.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal (eastward) direction, the horizontal component is obtained using the cosine of the angle.

The relationship is:

Horizontal component = V cos θ

where:

• V = magnitude of the velocity
• θ = angle measured from the horizontal direction

Cosine is used because the horizontal component is adjacent to the given angle.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Velocity, V = 22 m s⁻¹

Angle, θ = 30°

Step 2: Determine the required quantity.

The question asks for the eastward (horizontal) component of the velocity.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Horizontal component = V cos θ

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Horizontal component = 22 × cos 30°

Step 5: Use the trigonometric value.

cos 30° ≈ 0.866

Step 6: Perform the calculation.

Horizontal component = 22 × 0.866

= 19.052 m s⁻¹

≈ 19.1 m s⁻¹

Step 7: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated value is 19.1 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (11.0 m s⁻¹) is obtained by incorrectly using the sine function instead of the cosine function. This value represents the northward component, not the eastward component.
Option C (22.0 m s⁻¹) is the magnitude of the original velocity vector. A component cannot equal the original vector unless the angle is 0°.
Option D (25.4 m s⁻¹) is impossible because a component of a vector cannot be greater than the magnitude of the vector itself.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always identify the direction from which the angle is measured before choosing a trigonometric ratio.

• If the angle is measured from the horizontal, use cosine for the horizontal component and sine for the vertical component.

• If the angle is measured from the vertical, the roles of sine and cosine are reversed.

One of the most common JAMB mistakes is using the wrong trigonometric ratio.
explanation.final_answer: B. 19.1 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine looking at the ferry from above. Although it moves diagonally, part of its motion is directly towards the east and the other part is towards the north.

The eastward part is called the horizontal component. Since the angle is measured from the eastward direction, we multiply the speed by cos 30°.

22 × 0.866 ≈ 19.1 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the eastward component of the ferry's velocity is 19.1 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how to resolve vectors into horizontal and vertical components, choose the correct trigonometric ratio, and solve transportation, engineering, marine, and JAMB-style application problems with fully worked examples.

question:
While inspecting the support frame of an inclined solar panel, an engineer explains that the weight of the panel can be resolved into components acting parallel and perpendicular to the surface.

Assertion (A): Resolving a vector into perpendicular components makes it easier to analyse the effect of the vector along chosen directions.

Reason (R): The perpendicular components obtained from vector resolution together are equivalent to the original vector.

Which of the following options is correct?

options.A: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.
options.B: Both Assertion and Reason are true, but the Reason does not correctly explain the Assertion.
options.C: The Assertion is true, but the Reason is false.
options.D: The Assertion is false, but the Reason is true.

answer_text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of why vectors are resolved into components and the relationship between the original vector and its resolved components using an assertion–reason format.
explanation.principle:
Vector resolution is the process of splitting a single vector into two or more components acting along specified directions, usually at right angles (perpendicular) to one another.

The purpose of resolving a vector is not to change the vector itself but to simplify calculations. In many physics problems, motion or forces are analysed separately in mutually perpendicular directions because these directions can be treated independently.

The resolved components combine vectorially to reproduce exactly the original vector. They are therefore not new or unrelated vectors but parts of the same vector.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the Assertion.

The assertion states that resolving a vector into perpendicular components makes it easier to analyse the effect of the vector along chosen directions.

This is correct.

For example, on an inclined surface, a force or weight is often resolved into one component parallel to the slope and another perpendicular to it. Each component can then be analysed separately, making calculations much simpler.

Step 2: Examine the Reason.

The reason states that the perpendicular components obtained from vector resolution together are equivalent to the original vector.

This statement is also correct.

When the horizontal and vertical (or parallel and perpendicular) components are combined using vector addition, they reproduce the original vector exactly.

Step 3: Determine whether the Reason explains the Assertion.

The reason explains why vector resolution is useful. Since the components collectively represent the original vector, analysing the components individually gives the same physical result as analysing the original vector directly, but with greater simplicity.

Therefore, the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Both statements are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.

Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the Reason does explain why resolving a vector simplifies analysis. The two statements are logically connected.
Option C is incorrect because the Reason is true. The resolved components always combine to produce the original vector.
Option D is incorrect because the Assertion is also true. Resolving vectors is a standard technique used to simplify the analysis of forces, velocities and other vector quantities.
common_misconceptions: Some students think vector resolution changes the magnitude or direction of the original vector. It does not; it simply expresses the same vector in terms of components.
Another common misconception is that the resolved components are independent vectors unrelated to the original vector. In reality, they are parts of the original vector.
Some learners also believe vector resolution applies only to forces. It can be applied to any vector quantity, including displacement, velocity, acceleration and momentum.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB assertion–reason questions, first decide whether each statement is true before checking whether the Reason actually explains the Assertion. Two true statements do not automatically form a correct explanation.

Remember that vector resolution simplifies calculations because perpendicular components can be analysed independently and then combined to recover the original vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a heavy box using a rope inclined upward.

Instead of studying the pull as one slanted force, you split it into two parts: one pulling the box forward and the other lifting it slightly upward.

These two parts together still represent the original pull. This is why resolving vectors makes physics problems much easier to solve.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn why vectors are resolved into perpendicular components, how horizontal and vertical components are obtained, common examination mistakes, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries and additional practice questions.

question:
During the installation of a mining conveyor system, a tension of 110 N is applied to a supporting cable inclined at 30° above the horizontal. To determine the load acting upward on the support, the engineer resolves the tension into horizontal and vertical components.

What is the magnitude of the vertical component of the tension?

(Take sin 30° = 0.5.)

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to resolve an inclined force into its perpendicular components. Specifically, you are required to determine the vertical component of a tension acting at an angle above the horizontal in a mining conveyor installation.
explanation.principle:
Many forces encountered in engineering and physics do not act purely horizontally or vertically. Such forces are called inclined forces because they act at an angle.

To understand how an inclined force affects an object, it is often divided into two perpendicular parts called components:

• Horizontal component
• Vertical component

This process is known as **resolution of vectors**.

A beginner may ask, 'Why do we split one force into two components?'

The reason is that horizontal and vertical motions or effects can be analysed independently. Although there is only one original force, its influence can be represented by two mutually perpendicular components whose combined effect is exactly equal to the original force.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = Fcosθ
• Vertical component = Fsinθ

where:

• F = magnitude of the original force
• θ = angle the force makes with the horizontal

The sine function is used for the vertical component because, in the right-angled triangle formed during vector resolution, the vertical side is opposite the given angle.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Original tension, F = 110 N

Angle with the horizontal, θ = 30°

sin30° = 0.5

Step 2: Identify the quantity required.

Required: Vertical component of the tension.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Since the angle is measured from the horizontal:

Vertical component = Fsinθ

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Vertical component = 110 × 0.5

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Vertical component = 55 N

Step 6: Verify the answer.

The vertical component must be smaller than the original force because it is only one part of the inclined tension.

Since 55 N is less than 110 N, the result is physically reasonable.

Therefore, the correct answer is **55 N**.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (95.3 N) is incorrect because it is obtained by using 110cos30°, which gives the horizontal component, not the vertical component.
Option C (63.5 N) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error. It is not the vertical component for the given values.
Option D (110 N) is incorrect because it is the magnitude of the original tension. A component of a vector cannot equal the original vector unless the force acts entirely in that direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always determine the reference direction before choosing the trigonometric ratio.

• If the angle is measured from the horizontal:
  - Horizontal component = Fcosθ
  - Vertical component = Fsinθ

• If the angle is measured from the vertical:
  - Horizontal component = Fsinθ
  - Vertical component = Fcosθ

One of the most common JAMB mistakes is using sine and cosine interchangeably without checking where the angle is measured from.
explanation.final_answer: A. 55 N
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a heavy conveyor cable upward at an angle. Part of your pulling force moves the cable horizontally, while another part lifts it upward.

The upward lifting part is the vertical component.

Since the cable makes an angle of 30° with the horizontal, the vertical component is found using:

Vertical component = Force × sin30°

= 110 × 0.5

= 55 N.

Therefore, the upward force acting on the support is **55 N**.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Resolution of Vectors to learn how inclined vectors are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, how to select the correct trigonometric ratio based on the reference angle, and how these principles are applied in engineering, transportation, mining, construction and other JAMB-style scenarios. Continue practising with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused exercises.

question:
During the design of a pedestrian bridge, an engineer represents a supporting cable by a force of 50 N acting at an angle of 37° above the horizontal.

Which pair correctly represents the horizontal and vertical components of the force?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret the horizontal and vertical components of a vector when its magnitude and direction are known. Rather than finding the magnitude of the vector, you are required to identify the correct pair of perpendicular components.
explanation.principle:
Many physical quantities such as force, velocity and displacement are vectors because they possess both magnitude and direction.

A vector can often be resolved into two mutually perpendicular components:

• Horizontal component (along the x-axis)
• Vertical component (along the y-axis)

These components are not separate forces acting independently. Instead, they are two perpendicular vectors whose combined effect is exactly the same as the original vector.

When the angle is measured from the horizontal:

Horizontal component = Fcosθ

Vertical component = Fsinθ

where:

F = magnitude of the vector
θ = angle measured from the horizontal.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Magnitude of the force, F = 50 N

Angle with the horizontal, θ = 37°.

Step 2: Determine the horizontal component.

Horizontal component = Fcosθ

= 50 × cos37°

Using cos37° ≈ 0.80,

Horizontal component ≈ 50 × 0.80 = 40 N.

Step 3: Determine the vertical component.

Vertical component = Fsinθ

= 50 × sin37°

Using sin37° ≈ 0.60,

Vertical component ≈ 50 × 0.60 = 30 N.

Step 4: Compare the calculated values with the options.

The correct pair is 40 N horizontally and 30 N vertically.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B interchanges the horizontal and vertical components. Since the angle is measured from the horizontal, the horizontal component must be obtained using cosine, making it larger than the vertical component in this case.
Option C incorrectly treats the angle value as a component of the vector. An angle measured in degrees cannot be used directly as a force component.
Option D wrongly assigns the entire vector magnitude as one component and the angle as the other. A component cannot be greater than the original vector, and an angle is not a force.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always identify the reference axis before resolving a vector.

• If the angle is measured from the horizontal, use cosine for the horizontal component and sine for the vertical component.

• If the angle is measured from the vertical, the roles of sine and cosine are reversed.

A common JAMB mistake is to interchange these components without first checking the reference axis.
explanation.final_answer: A. 40 N horizontally and 30 N vertically
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pulling a cable diagonally.

Part of the pull acts horizontally, while the remaining part acts vertically. These two perpendicular components work together to produce the original diagonal force.

Since the cable makes an angle with the horizontal, the horizontal component is found using cosine and the vertical component using sine. This gives 40 N horizontally and 30 N vertically.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components to learn how vectors are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, how the reference axis affects the choice of sine or cosine, and how these ideas are applied in engineering, mechanics and JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and additional practice questions.

question:
An emergency rescue drone flies such that its displacement consists of a horizontal component of 24 m and a vertical component of 32 m.

What is the magnitude of the drone's resultant displacement?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the magnitude of a vector when its horizontal and vertical components are known. It applies the concept of vector components in the context of an emergency rescue drone.
explanation.principle:
A vector component is one of the perpendicular parts into which a vector is divided.

For example, a drone moving through the air may travel both horizontally and vertically at the same time. Instead of analysing the complete motion directly, we split the displacement into two perpendicular components:

• Horizontal component
• Vertical component

These two components form the perpendicular sides of a right-angled triangle, while the original displacement is the hypotenuse.

To recover the original vector from its components, we use Pythagoras' theorem:

Magnitude of vector = √[(Horizontal component)² + (Vertical component)²]

This relationship works because the components are at right angles (90°) to each other.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Horizontal component = 24 m
Vertical component = 32 m

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Required: Magnitude of the resultant displacement.

Step 3: Write the appropriate formula.

Resultant magnitude = √(Horizontal² + Vertical²)

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Resultant = √(24² + 32²)
= √(576 + 1024)
= √1600

Step 5: Evaluate the square root.

√1600 = 40

Therefore, the magnitude of the drone's displacement is 40 m.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (56 m) is obtained by simply adding the two components (24 + 32). Vector components that are perpendicular must not be added directly when determining the vector's magnitude.
Option C (8 m) results from subtracting the components (32 − 24). Subtraction does not give the magnitude of the original vector in this situation.
Option D (768 m) comes from multiplying the components (24 × 32). Multiplication has no physical meaning in determining the magnitude of a vector from perpendicular components.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question gives horizontal and vertical components of a vector, immediately think of a right-angled triangle.

Remember:

• Components are perpendicular.
• Use Pythagoras' theorem to determine the original vector.
• Never add or subtract perpendicular components directly when finding the magnitude.

JAMB frequently tests this concept using displacement, velocity, force and acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: A. 40 m
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the drone first moves 24 m horizontally and then rises 32 m vertically.

Its actual path is the straight-line distance from the starting point to its final position. Since the horizontal and vertical movements are perpendicular, they form a right-angled triangle.

Using Pythagoras' theorem:

24² + 32² = 576 + 1024 = 1600

√1600 = 40 m

Therefore, the drone's resultant displacement is 40 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components to learn how vectors are resolved into perpendicular components, how to determine the magnitude and direction of a vector from its components, and how these ideas are applied in engineering, aviation, navigation and JAMB examination questions.

question:
During a Physics lesson, a teacher explained that any vector can be resolved into two perpendicular components along mutually perpendicular axes.

Why are the components chosen to be perpendicular to each other?

options.A: Because perpendicular components can be treated independently and combined to reproduce the original vector accurately.
options.B: Because perpendicular components always have equal magnitudes.
options.C: Because every vector naturally acts only in vertical and horizontal directions.
options.D: Because using perpendicular components increases the magnitude of the original vector.

answer_text: Because perpendicular components can be treated independently and combined to reproduce the original vector accurately.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of why vector components are resolved along two perpendicular directions rather than along any two arbitrary directions.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. In Physics, a vector can be represented by two independent components that act at right angles (90°) to each other.

These are usually the horizontal (x-axis) and vertical (y-axis) components.

A beginner may ask, 'Why must the components be perpendicular?'

Perpendicular directions are mathematically independent. This means that motion or force in one direction does not directly affect the other. Because of this independence, each component can be analysed separately before they are combined to recover the original vector.

This approach greatly simplifies calculations in mechanics, engineering, navigation, electricity and many other areas of Physics.
steps:
Step 1: Recall the meaning of vector components.

Vector components are smaller vectors whose combined effect is exactly the same as the original vector.

Step 2: Understand why perpendicular directions are preferred.

When two components are at right angles (90°), each represents an independent effect along its own axis. This independence allows each component to be calculated separately.

Step 3: Examine Option A.

This correctly explains that perpendicular components can be analysed independently and later combined to reproduce the original vector.

Therefore, Option A is correct.

Step 4: Examine the remaining options.

They each contain misconceptions about vector components and do not explain the real reason for choosing perpendicular axes.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because perpendicular components do not have to be equal in magnitude. Their values depend on the direction of the original vector.
Option C is incorrect because a vector may point in any direction. Horizontal and vertical directions are simply convenient reference axes for analysis.
Option D is incorrect because resolving a vector into components does not increase its magnitude. The components combine vectorially to reproduce exactly the original vector.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about vector components, remember that the horizontal and vertical components are chosen because they are perpendicular and therefore independent. This makes calculations involving forces, displacement and velocity much easier.

Do not confuse 'perpendicular components' with 'equal components.' They are not necessarily the same.
explanation.final_answer: A. Because perpendicular components can be treated independently and combined to reproduce the original vector accurately.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a box across a floor while also lifting it slightly. The forward push and the upward lift act in different directions and do not interfere with each other.

Physicists treat these two directions separately because they are perpendicular. After analysing each one, they combine them to obtain the overall effect. This is why vector components are chosen to be at right angles.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components to learn why vectors are resolved into perpendicular components, how horizontal and vertical components are obtained, and how they are applied in mechanics, engineering and navigation. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples and JAMB-focused practice.

question: An airport radar detects an aircraft moving with a velocity of 85 m s⁻¹. If its horizontal component of velocity is 36 m s⁻¹, what is the vertical component of its velocity?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the missing vertical component of an aircraft's velocity when the total velocity (resultant vector) and the horizontal component are known.
explanation.principle:
A vector can be resolved into two mutually perpendicular components, usually the horizontal and vertical components.

These components combine to produce the original vector. Since they are at right angles to each other, they form a right-angled triangle.

The relationship between the vector and its components is given by the Pythagorean theorem:

V² = Vₓ² + Vᵧ²

where:
• V is the magnitude of the original vector.
• Vₓ is the horizontal component.
• Vᵧ is the vertical component.

Whenever the resultant and one component are known, the missing component can be obtained by rearranging the formula.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

Magnitude of velocity, V = 85 m s⁻¹
Horizontal component, Vₓ = 36 m s⁻¹
Vertical component, Vᵧ = ?

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

V² = Vₓ² + Vᵧ²

Rearranging,

Vᵧ = √(V² − Vₓ²)

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Vᵧ = √(85² − 36²)

= √(7225 − 1296)

= √5929

Step 4: Evaluate the square root.

√5929 = 77

Therefore, the vertical component is 77 m s⁻¹.

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

Option B matches the calculated value.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (49 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It may result from an arithmetic error when subtracting the squared values.
Option C (93 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because a component of a vector cannot be greater than the magnitude of the vector itself.
Option D (121 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it could result from adding the squared quantities instead of taking the square root correctly.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a vector is resolved into horizontal and vertical components, remember that the components are perpendicular.

Do not subtract the magnitudes directly. Always apply the Pythagorean theorem using the squared values before taking the square root.

Also check that the calculated component is less than the magnitude of the original vector.
explanation.final_answer: B. 77 m s⁻¹
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the aircraft's velocity as the longest side of a right-angled triangle. The horizontal and vertical components form the other two sides.

Since one side and the longest side are known, you can use Pythagoras' theorem to find the remaining side.

This gives a vertical component of 77 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components for detailed lessons on resolving vectors, determining unknown components, using the Pythagorean theorem, and solving JAMB-style application questions involving velocity, force and displacement.

question:
Two mobile robots are programmed to move across a factory floor.

Robot P has horizontal and vertical velocity components of 18 m s⁻¹ and 24 m s⁻¹ respectively.

Robot Q has horizontal and vertical velocity components of 15 m s⁻¹ and 20 m s⁻¹ respectively.

Which of the following statements correctly compares the motions of the two robots?

options.A: Robot P has the greater speed because both of its velocity components are larger than those of Robot Q.
options.B: Both robots have the same speed because they move in the same general direction.
options.C: Robot Q has the greater speed because its horizontal and vertical components are more nearly equal.
options.D: The speeds of the robots cannot be compared unless the angles of motion are known.

answer_text: Robot P has the greater speed because both of its velocity components are larger than those of Robot Q.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to compare two vectors using their horizontal and vertical components rather than by looking at only one component or requiring the direction angles.
explanation.principle:
A vector can be represented by two perpendicular components: a horizontal component and a vertical component.

These components combine to produce the original vector.

The magnitude (or size) of a vector is obtained using Pythagoras' theorem:

Vector magnitude = √[(horizontal component)² + (vertical component)²]

Whenever both perpendicular components of one vector are greater than the corresponding components of another vector, the first vector must also have the greater magnitude.

Therefore, comparing components is often sufficient to compare the magnitudes of vectors without first determining their directions.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Robot P.

Horizontal component = 18 m s⁻¹

Vertical component = 24 m s⁻¹

Magnitude = √(18² + 24²)

= √(324 + 576)

= √900

= 30 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Examine Robot Q.

Horizontal component = 15 m s⁻¹

Vertical component = 20 m s⁻¹

Magnitude = √(15² + 20²)

= √(225 + 400)

= √625

= 25 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Compare the magnitudes.

Robot P has a speed of 30 m s⁻¹, while Robot Q has a speed of 25 m s⁻¹.

Since 30 m s⁻¹ is greater than 25 m s⁻¹, Robot P moves faster.

Step 4: Select the correct statement.

Option A correctly states that Robot P has the greater speed because both of its velocity components are larger, leading to a larger resultant velocity.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because moving in roughly the same direction does not imply equal speed. Speed depends on the magnitude of the velocity vector.
Option C is incorrect because having components that are closer in value does not necessarily produce a larger vector magnitude. The actual sizes of the components determine the magnitude.
Option D is incorrect because the speeds can be determined directly from the components using Pythagoras' theorem. The direction angles are not required for this comparison.
common_misconceptions: Believing that vectors can only be compared if their directions are known.
Assuming that one larger component alone guarantees a larger vector.
Thinking that vectors with similar component values must have equal magnitudes.
Comparing only the horizontal or only the vertical component instead of considering both together.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB, when two vectors are expressed in terms of perpendicular components, compare both components carefully. If necessary, calculate the resultant magnitude using Pythagoras' theorem before drawing a conclusion. Never compare vectors using only one component.
explanation.final_answer: A. Robot P has the greater speed because both of its velocity components are larger than those of Robot Q.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine each robot moves partly sideways and partly forward.

Its overall speed depends on the combination of both movements.

Robot P moves faster sideways and faster forward than Robot Q, so its overall speed is also greater.

Calculating the magnitudes confirms this:

Robot P = 30 m s⁻¹

Robot Q = 25 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components to learn how horizontal and vertical components combine to produce the original vector, how to compare vectors using their components, and how these ideas are applied in robotics, engineering, navigation and JAMB examination questions.

question:
An autonomous delivery vehicle is programmed to move with a velocity of 25 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 37° north of east while travelling across a large industrial facility.

For its navigation software, the velocity must be expressed as horizontal and vertical components.

Using cos 37° = 0.80 and sin 37° = 0.60, what are the horizontal and vertical components of the vehicle's velocity respectively?

options.A: 20 m s⁻¹ east and 15 m s⁻¹ north
options.B: 15 m s⁻¹ east and 20 m s⁻¹ north
options.C: 25 m s⁻¹ east and 15 m s⁻¹ north
options.D: 20 m s⁻¹ east and 25 m s⁻¹ north

answer_text: 20 m s⁻¹ east and 15 m s⁻¹ north

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to resolve the velocity of an autonomous vehicle into its horizontal (eastward) and vertical (northward) components using trigonometric ratios.
explanation.principle:
Many physical quantities such as displacement, velocity, force and acceleration are vectors because they possess both magnitude and direction.

A vector can be represented by two perpendicular components. These components together produce exactly the same effect as the original vector.

When a vector makes an angle with the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = V cos θ
• Vertical component = V sin θ

where:

V = magnitude of the vector
θ = angle measured from the horizontal.

Using vector components allows engineers, robotic systems and navigation software to analyse motion independently along the horizontal and vertical directions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Magnitude of velocity, V = 25 m s⁻¹
Angle, θ = 37° north of east
cos 37° = 0.80
sin 37° = 0.60

Step 2: Calculate the horizontal component.

Horizontal component = V cos θ
= 25 × 0.80
= 20 m s⁻¹ east

Step 3: Calculate the vertical component.

Vertical component = V sin θ
= 25 × 0.60
= 15 m s⁻¹ north

Step 4: Compare the result with the options.

The correct pair is 20 m s⁻¹ east and 15 m s⁻¹ north.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it interchanges the horizontal and vertical components. Since the angle is measured from the horizontal (east), cosine gives the horizontal component and sine gives the vertical component.
Option C is incorrect because it incorrectly assumes the original vector magnitude is the horizontal component. A component can never exceed the magnitude of the original vector.
Option D is incorrect because it treats the original vector magnitude as the vertical component, which is impossible since the components together combine to produce the original vector.
explanation.exam_tip:
Always identify the reference axis before choosing between sine and cosine.

If the angle is measured from the horizontal:

• Horizontal component = V cos θ
• Vertical component = V sin θ

If the angle is measured from the vertical, the roles of sine and cosine are reversed.

One of the most common JAMB mistakes is using the correct formula with the wrong reference axis.
explanation.final_answer: A. 20 m s⁻¹ east and 15 m s⁻¹ north
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine looking at the vehicle's movement from above. Although it travels in a slanting direction, its motion can be split into two simpler movements:

• one towards the east,
• one towards the north.

These two component motions work together to produce the actual path of the vehicle. Here, the eastward motion is 20 m s⁻¹, while the northward motion is 15 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Scalars and Vectors → Vector Components to learn how vectors are resolved into horizontal and vertical components, how the reference axis determines the correct trigonometric ratio, and how these ideas are applied in robotics, autonomous navigation, aviation and engineering through detailed JAMB-style worked examples.

question:
While reviewing an engineering laboratory exercise, an instructor noticed that a trainee made the following conclusion:

'A vector can be replaced by any two smaller vectors, even if they are not perpendicular, because vector components simply divide the original vector into parts.' 

Which aspect of the trainee's reasoning is incorrect?

options.A: Vector components must always be perpendicular to one another for the chosen coordinate axes.
options.B: A vector can only be resolved into components if its magnitude is unknown.
options.C: The sum of the magnitudes of the components must always equal the magnitude of the original vector.
options.D: Only force vectors can be resolved into components.

answer_text: Vector components must always be perpendicular to one another for the chosen coordinate axes.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to identify a common misconception about vector components. Rather than performing a calculation, you must determine why the trainee's explanation of vector components is scientifically incorrect.
explanation.principle:
A vector is a quantity that possesses both magnitude and direction. Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration, force and momentum.

Very often, it is easier to analyse a vector by splitting it into two mutually perpendicular components. These are called vector components.

A beginner may ask, 'Why are the components perpendicular?'

In elementary Physics, vectors are usually resolved along two coordinate axes that meet at right angles, such as the horizontal (x-axis) and vertical (y-axis). These perpendicular components combine vectorially to reproduce exactly the original vector.

Although a vector may be expressed in different coordinate systems, the standard vector components used for analysis are taken along perpendicular axes because this greatly simplifies calculations in mechanics, engineering and navigation.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the trainee's statement.
